liquidation_preference.notes: Participating preferred occasionally appears in distressed/down rounds but remains rare.

anti_dilution.full_ratchet: Effectively extinct in standard US venture. Appears only in extreme distress scenarios.

standard_set_covers: Issuance of senior or pari passu securities

board_composition_by_stage.late_stage: 7-9 members, trend toward board size compression

pay_to_play.prevalence: 6-10% of deals (peaked 10.1% Q3 2025, down to 6.3% Q4 2025)
pay_to_play.notes: Historically rare (<3%) but elevated in current market. Cooley tracks quarterly.

pre_seed.governance: Minimal — no board seats, no protective provisions
pre_seed.key_issues: Cap/discount clarity, MFN provisions, pro rata at conversion

seed.governance: Board observer common; seat rare unless sole lead
seed.key_issues: Option pool sizing, SAFE stacking risk, founder vesting

series_a.governance: First investor board seat typical, protective provisions standard
series_a.key_issues: Full doc suite negotiation, board dynamics, option pool refresh

series_b.governance: Second investor seat common, enhanced information rights
series_b.key_issues: Pro rata allocation, MFN across investor classes, drag-along thresholds

growth_stage.governance: Multiple investor seats, independent director requirements
key_issues: Structured terms increase (participating pref, ratchets, IPO protections)

late_stage_pre_ipo.governance: Complex multi-class board, audit committee requirements
key_issues: IPO ratchets and minimum return guarantees

training_data_rights: Thomson Reuters v. Ross Intelligence (Feb 2025): court rejected fair use for AI training.
Anthropic copyright settlement: pirated training data held 'inherently infringing'.
Data licensing obligations are now standard diligence items.
model_weights: Microsoft-OpenAI: model weights explicitly carved out of Microsoft's IP rights.
Separate IP assignment representations needed for model artifacts vs code.
employee_mobility: Trade secret litigation surging between AI labs (xAI v. OpenAI, Google/Ding prosecution).
Non-compete enforceability varies by state (CA ban, other states mixed).
open_source_licensing: Llama 'community license' is NOT true open-source (700M MAU threshold, derivative ownership retained).
Apache 2.0 and MIT remain clean for commercial use.
Protective provisions should address consent before open-sourcing proprietary models.

compute_and_infrastructure.gpu_as_collateral: GPU-backed debt market exceeds $20B. CoreWeave pledged H100s for $2.3B.
compute_and_infrastructure.cloud_credits: Increasingly blended into headline round sizes (OpenAI $110B round mixes cash and cloud).
compute_and_infrastructure.depreciation: Hyperscalers extended GPU depreciation from 3-4 years to 6 years; economic depreciation is 30-40% in year one.

regulatory.eu_ai_act: In force Aug 2024. Applies to US companies serving EU markets. Classification tiers with compliance obligations.
regulatory.us_executive_orders: Biden EO 14110 revoked by Trump. Current posture is deregulation-focused.
regulatory.export_controls: BIS restricts closed AI model weights. Dual-use reporting threshold: >10^26 ops training runs.
regulatory.itar_ear: AI systems may fall under dual-use controls depending on application domain.

valuation.multiples: Average AI revenue multiple 23.4x (2024-2025), but highly bifurcated.
valuation.infrastructure_vs_application: LLM vendors and infra command premiums; applied AI converging to SaaS benchmarks.
valuation.wrapper_compression: AI wrapper valuations collapsed from 9-12x to 3-4x ARR.
valuation.gross_margins: AI companies typically 50-60% vs 80-90% for traditional SaaS due to compute costs.

emerging_provisions: Open-sourcing restrictions: investor consent before releasing model weights.
Training data licensing representations in reps and warranties.
Model safety commitments in governance documents.
PBC structures creating novel governance dynamics (OpenAI Foundation retains board power despite 26% equity).
diligence_additions: Training data provenance and licensing audit.
Compute supply chain analysis (GPU access, cloud commitments).
Employee invention assignment coverage for model artifacts.
Open-source component analysis for model dependencies.

sec_landscape_2025.policy_shift: Gensler enforcement era (83+ actions) replaced by Atkins rules-based framework.
sec_landscape_2025.crypto_task_force: Led by Hester Peirce. Focus on regulatory clarity over enforcement.
sec_landscape_2025.token_safe_harbor: Proposal 3.0 under consideration: three-year grace period for decentralization.

cftc.spot_trading: Spot crypto trading now permitted on CFTC-regulated exchanges (first time ever).
cftc.tokenized_collateral: Stablecoins and tokenized Treasuries allowed in derivatives markets.

sanctions_ofac.general: OFAC obligations identical for crypto and fiat (FAQ 560).
sanctions_ofac.tornado_cash: Fifth Circuit ruled immutable smart contracts are NOT property under IEEPA (Nov 2024). OFAC revoked designation April 2025.
sanctions_ofac.oisp_part_850: Effective Jan 2, 2025. Restricts US outbound investments in AI/semiconductors/quantum in China/HK/Macau.

international.mica: Fully operational Dec 30, 2024 across 27 EU states. 40+ CASP licenses issued.
international.uk_fca: Comprehensive consultation papers published Dec 2025. Full rules expected by Oct 2027.
international.singapore_mas: Finalized stablecoin framework. Preferred crypto incorporation jurisdiction.

token_warrant_norms.structure: SAFE + Token Warrant hybrid is dominant 2024-2025 structure.
token_warrant_norms.net_exercise: Standard (pay on paper, receive net tokens).
token_warrant_norms.lockup_periods: Institutional standard 1-4 year schedule with 6-12 month cliff.
token_warrant_norms.initial_circulation_at_tge: 5-15% of total supply. >25% unlock in 90 days creates 2-4x sell pressure.

delaware_quirks.deemed_liquidation: TGE may trigger deemed liquidation event under standard preferred provisions. Must be explicitly carved out in charter.
delaware_quirks.section_220: Books-and-records rights apply to token records (Switchboard case, March 2025).
delaware_quirks.jurisdiction: Delaware cannot assert in rem jurisdiction over crypto based on title location alone (2025 Chancery).
delaware_quirks.dgcl_122_18: 2024 amendments enable stockholder agreements relevant to token governance.

dao_governance.lido_dao_ruling: Nov 2024: VC firms face unlimited personal liability for active DAO governance participation.
dao_governance.unincorporated_risk: Default general partnership with pass-through taxation to all token holders.

precedent_cases_2024_2025.holding: Secondary market sales not securities; programmatic sales may be.
SEC enforcement dismissed under new administration.
VC firms face GP liability for active DAO governance.
Immutable smart contracts not 'property' under IEEPA.
DGCL Section 220 applies to token records.

defined_terms_reference.purpose: Common NVCA defined terms, their variations across documents, economic mechanics, governance implications, and cross-document consistency traps.

major_investor.definition: An investor meeting a minimum ownership or investment threshold, qualifying for enhanced rights gated behind this status.
major_investor.where_defined: Investors' Rights Agreement (IRA), typically cross-referenced in ROFR/Co-Sale Agreement.

dollar_amount.description: Minimum aggregate purchase price of Registrable Securities held.
dollar_amount.trap: Dollar thresholds can become meaningless after multiple rounds at different prices. An investor who bought $250K at $1/share holds a very different position than one who bought $250K at $10/share.

share_count.description: Minimum number of shares of Registrable Securities held.
share_count.trap: Share count thresholds don't adjust for stock splits or recapitalizations unless the definition explicitly includes adjustment language.

rights_gated: Pro rata rights (right to participate in future rounds)
Board observer rights (sometimes gated here, sometimes separate)
major_investor.cross_document_trap: The MI threshold in the IRA (governing information/pro-rata rights) may differ from the threshold in the ROFR/Co-Sale Agreement (governing transfer rights). An investor can qualify as a Major Investor for information purposes but not for ROFR participation, or vice versa. Always verify the definition is identical or intentionally different across all documents.
negotiation_notes: Founders prefer higher thresholds to reduce the number of investors with enhanced rights and administrative burden.
Lead investors want lower thresholds for co-investors to ensure broad syndicate alignment.
Angel investors argue high thresholds unfairly penalize early backers who took the most risk.

preferred_majority.definition: Holders of a majority (>50%) of the outstanding shares of Preferred Stock, voting together as a single class on an as-converted basis.
preferred_majority.typical_usage: Protective provisions in the Certificate of Incorporation, amendment/waiver of transaction documents.
preferred_majority.trap: When all series vote as one class, the largest series (typically the latest round) dominates. Series A holders may be unable to block actions despite protective provisions nominally requiring 'Preferred Majority' approval.

requisite_holders.definition: A defined subset of holders whose approval is required for specific actions. May require approval of each series separately, or approval of holders of a specified percentage of a particular series.
requisite_holders.typical_usage: Series-specific protective provisions, IRA amendments, waiver of particular rights.
variants: Holders of [X]% of Series [_] Preferred, voting as a separate class
Holders of a majority of each series of Preferred, each voting as a separate class
Holders of [X]% of Preferred + consent of [Lead Investor]
requisite_holders.trap: If the charter uses 'Preferred Majority' (all series as one class) but the IRA defines 'Requisite Holders' as requiring each series separately, the same action may be approvable under one document and blocked under another.

preferred_majority_vs_requisite_holders.key_distinction: Preferred Majority is a single-class aggregate vote; Requisite Holders may require series-by-series consent. The choice determines whether later-round investors can override earlier investors.

deemed_liquidation_event.definition: A corporate transaction that is treated as a liquidation for purposes of triggering the preferred stock liquidation preference waterfall, even though the company continues to exist.

standard_triggers.description: Corporation is a constituent party and stockholders before the transaction hold less than 50% of voting power after.
standard_triggers.trap: Subsidiary mergers may or may not trigger DLE depending on whether the parent issues stock. Triangular mergers require careful drafting.

standard_triggers.description: Sale, lease, transfer, or other disposition of all or substantially all assets of the corporation.
standard_triggers.trap: 'Substantially all' is not a bright line. Delaware courts use qualitative and quantitative tests. A sale of 51% of assets by value may or may not qualify.

standard_triggers.description: Grant of an exclusive license of all or substantially all intellectual property.
standard_triggers.trap: This is the most contested trigger. Companies argue an exclusive license is not a transfer of ownership. Investors argue it has the economic effect of an asset sale. Threshold is typically 'substantially all' IP, but some drafts use revenue-based tests (e.g., IP generating >50% of revenue).

standard_triggers.event: Statutory conversion, transfer, domestication, or continuance
standard_triggers.description: Added in Oct 2025 NVCA update. Covers redomiciliation transactions that could restructure preferred rights.
standard_triggers.trap: New trigger—older documents may not include this, creating a gap if the company redomiciliates.

opt_out_mechanics.description: Holders of a specified percentage of Preferred Stock may elect to opt out of DLE treatment by written notice before the effective date.
opt_out_mechanics.notice_period: Typically 10-20 days prior to effective date.
opt_out_mechanics.redemption_fallback: If not dissolved within 90 days, company must offer to redeem preferred at liquidation amount. DLE Redemption Date is 150 days after event (Oct 2025 update).

deemed_liquidation_event.crypto_trap: Token generation events (TGEs) may inadvertently trigger DLE provisions. Must be explicitly carved out in the charter for crypto/token companies.

qualified_ipo.definition: An initial public offering meeting specified minimum thresholds, triggering automatic conversion of all Preferred Stock into Common Stock.

minimum_gross_proceeds.trap: Net vs. gross matters. Some definitions specify 'gross proceeds net of underwriting discount and commissions,' which can be 7% lower. Others use pure gross proceeds.

minimum_per_share_price.description: Per-share offering price must equal or exceed a multiple of the Original Issue Price (commonly 2x-5x OIP of the latest series).
minimum_per_share_price.trap: Per-share price requirements protect against IPOs that technically raise the minimum proceeds but at a low valuation. However, stale OIP multiples from prior rounds can block otherwise reasonable IPOs.

underwriting_requirement.description: Most definitions require a 'firm commitment underwritten public offering,' excluding best-efforts offerings and direct listings.
underwriting_requirement.trap: Direct listings and SPACs may not qualify as a 'Qualified IPO' under standard definitions. Companies pursuing non-traditional paths to public markets must negotiate carveouts.

effects_of_qualification: Automatic conversion of all Preferred Stock to Common Stock
Termination of most transfer restriction agreements (ROFR, co-sale)
Termination of information rights (replaced by public company disclosure)
Registration rights survive and become exercisable

qualified_financing.definition: A subsequent equity financing round meeting minimum aggregate proceeds, typically triggering conversion of convertible instruments or pay-to-play obligations.

for_convertible_note_conversion.trap: If threshold is too low, a small insider round can trigger conversion at terms unfavorable to note holders. If too high, notes may never convert and mature into repayable debt.

for_pay_to_play.description: A subsequent financing approved by the board in which existing investors must participate pro rata to maintain preferred status.
for_pay_to_play.consequence: Non-participating investors have Preferred Stock converted to Common Stock, losing liquidation preference, anti-dilution, and protective provision rights.
for_pay_to_play.trap: Some definitions exclude bridge financings or insider rounds from 'Qualified Financing' for pay-to-play purposes. Others do not, allowing insiders to engineer a conversion event.

affiliate.definition: Any entity that directly or indirectly controls, is controlled by, or is under common control with, a specified person. 'Control' typically means ownership of >50% of voting securities or equivalent power to direct management.

breadth_variants.narrow: Direct subsidiaries and parent entities only (>50% ownership test).
breadth_variants.standard: Entities under common control, including fund affiliates (same GP managing multiple funds).
breadth_variants.broad: Includes portfolio companies of affiliated funds, advisory relationships, limited partners with board representation.
breadth_variants.trap: Broad affiliate definitions in transfer exceptions allow investors to freely transfer shares to any entity in their fund family without triggering ROFR. This can effectively circumvent transfer restrictions. Conversely, broad affiliate definitions in competitor carveouts can exclude large swaths of the market from non-compete protections.

key_cases: Sixth Street Partners v. Dyal Capital Partners (Del.): Transfer restrictions did not apply to upstream affiliates because contract language did not clearly encompass them.
Symbiont.io: Restrictive covenants enforced against non-party acquirers when affiliate definition was broad enough.

original_issue_price.definition: The per-share price at which a series of Preferred Stock was originally issued. Fixed at closing and never changes.
original_issue_price.where_defined: Certificate of Incorporation (typically in the definitions section of the preferred stock designation).

conversion_price.definition: The price used to calculate the conversion ratio (OIP / Conversion Price = shares of Common received per share of Preferred). Initially equal to OIP but subject to anti-dilution adjustment.

broad_based_weighted_average.description: Conversion Price adjusted using formula: CP2 = CP1 * (A + B) / (A + C), where A = pre-money fully diluted shares, B = shares purchasable at old CP, C = shares actually issued in dilutive round.
broad_based_weighted_average.trap: 'Broad-based' vs 'narrow-based' turns on what's included in 'A' (fully diluted denominator). Broad includes all options, warrants, convertible instruments. Narrow includes only outstanding shares. Narrow BBWA gives investors more anti-dilution protection.

full_ratchet.description: Conversion Price reset to the lowest price at which any shares are issued in a dilutive round. Maximum investor protection.
full_ratchet.prevalence: Effectively extinct in standard US venture (<1% of deals).
full_ratchet.trap: Even a small issuance at a low price (e.g., advisor shares) can reset the entire series conversion price.

conversion_price.key_distinction: OIP never changes; Conversion Price only goes down (never up). After a down round with BBWA adjustment, investors convert at a more favorable ratio, receiving more Common shares per Preferred share.

liquidation_amount.definition: The total amount a holder of Preferred Stock is entitled to receive per share before Common Stock holders receive anything upon a liquidation or Deemed Liquidation Event.
liquidation_amount.standard_formula: Liquidation Amount = [Multiple] x Original Issue Price + declared but unpaid dividends on such share.

multiple.elevated: 1.5x-3x (rare, seen in distressed/down rounds or late-stage structured deals)
multiple.trap: Multiples above 1x dramatically reduce common stock payouts. A 2x preference on a $50M investment means $100M must be distributed to that series before common holders see anything.

declared_but_unpaid_dividends.non_cumulative: Only dividends actually declared by the board but not yet paid are included. If the board never declares dividends (typical for startups), this adds $0.
declared_but_unpaid_dividends.cumulative: Dividends accrue whether or not declared, adding to the liquidation amount over time. Rare in venture but appears in structured deals.
declared_but_unpaid_dividends.trap: Cumulative dividends at 8% on a $100M investment add $8M/year to the liquidation stack. After 5 years, the preference has grown from $100M to $140M without any board action.

participating_vs_non_participating.non_participating: Investor chooses greater of: (a) Liquidation Amount, or (b) as-converted pro rata share. Cannot get both.
participating_vs_non_participating.participating: Investor gets Liquidation Amount PLUS pro rata share of remaining proceeds. The 'double dip.'
participating_vs_non_participating.participating_with_cap: Participating, but total payout capped at a multiple of OIP (e.g., 3x). Investor stops participating once cap is reached.
participating_vs_non_participating.trap: The participation/non-participation distinction is the single most impactful economic term after valuation. At moderate exit values, participating preferred can reduce founder proceeds by 30-50%.

distribution.definition: Any payment, dividend, or transfer of value from the corporation to its stockholders.

breadth_variants.narrow: Cash dividends and liquidation distributions only.
breadth_variants.standard: Cash dividends, property dividends, share repurchases, and liquidation distributions.
breadth_variants.broad: All of the above plus payments under consulting agreements, IP licenses to stockholder-affiliates, and any other transfer of value. May capture salary payments to founder-stockholders.
breadth_variants.trap: Broad distribution definitions can inadvertently trigger waterfall provisions on routine business transactions. Founder salary payments, technology licensing to an investor's portfolio company, or strategic partnerships may be recharacterized as distributions requiring preference satisfaction.

fully_diluted.definition: The total number of shares of Common Stock outstanding assuming conversion or exercise of all convertible securities, options, and warrants.

as_converted.description: All Preferred Stock converted at current Conversion Price + all outstanding options/warrants exercised + all convertible instruments converted. No offset for exercise proceeds.
as_converted.usage: Standard in venture capital charter documents, cap table calculations, and ownership percentage determinations.
as_converted.trap: Overstates dilution because it assumes all options are exercised regardless of whether they are in-the-money, and ignores exercise proceeds the company would receive.

treasury_method.description: Assumes in-the-money options/warrants exercised, proceeds used to repurchase shares at current fair market value. Only net incremental shares counted.
treasury_method.usage: GAAP diluted EPS calculations, some late-stage/pre-IPO financing documents.
treasury_method.trap: Understates dilution in private companies where there is no reliable market price for repurchase assumptions.

always_included: Vested and unvested options in ESOP
sometimes_included: Convertible notes and SAFEs (if conversion terms known)
rarely_included: Future option pool increases not yet authorized
what_counts.trap: Whether the unissued option pool reserve is included in fully diluted count dramatically affects ownership percentages. Including it dilutes existing holders; excluding it inflates their apparent ownership. The term sheet should specify explicitly.

rights: Attend board meetings (may be excluded from executive sessions)
Receive board materials and meeting packages
Ask questions and provide input during meetings

obligations.fiduciary_duties: None. Observer rights are contractual, not statutory. No duty of care or loyalty to the corporation.
obligations.confidentiality: Bound only by contractual NDA/confidentiality provisions in the IRA or side letter, not by statutory duties.

indemnification.status: Typically NOT covered by standard D&O insurance or indemnification agreements.
indemnification.trap: Observers seeking indemnification may be deemed to be acting as de facto directors, triggering fiduciary duties they were trying to avoid. Some practitioners argue requesting indemnification is inconsistent with observer-only status.

board_observer.exclusion_rights: Company typically reserves the right to exclude observers from portions of meetings where their presence would waive attorney-client privilege or create conflicts of interest.
board_observer.prevalence: 82% of VC funds utilize board observers. Common for non-lead investors and seed-stage leads.

rights: Full voting power on all board matters
Access to all board materials and company information
Participation in all board meetings including executive sessions

obligations.fiduciary_duties: Full duties of care and loyalty under Delaware law, including subsidiary duties of good faith, oversight (Caremark), and disclosure.
obligations.conflicts: Must recuse from conflicted transactions. Dual fiduciary issues when investor-designated director's fund interest conflicts with company interest.

board_director.indemnification: Standard D&O insurance and indemnification agreement coverage. NVCA model Indemnification Agreement covers directors.
board_director.trap: Investor-designated directors owe fiduciary duties to ALL stockholders, not just the designating investor. Voting in the investor's interest against the company's interest can create personal liability (In re Trados).

charter_based.location: Certificate of Incorporation, in the preferred stock designation.
charter_based.enforceability: Binding on the corporation and all stockholders as a matter of corporate law. Cannot be circumvented by board action alone.
standard_stockholder_level_provisions: Amend charter or bylaws in a manner adverse to Preferred Stock
Authorize or issue securities senior to or pari passu with existing Preferred
Increase or decrease authorized shares of Preferred Stock
Redeem or repurchase Common Stock (outside approved equity plans)
Declare or pay dividends on any stock
Effect any Deemed Liquidation Event (merger, asset sale, dissolution)
Increase or decrease the authorized size of the Board
Create any subsidiary or acquire assets above a threshold
charter_based.post_moelis_note: After Moelis v. Moelis (Del. Ch. Feb 2024), governance restrictions previously placed in stockholder agreements should be in the charter to avoid facial invalidity challenges. DGCL 122(18) (effective Aug 2024) provides a statutory safe harbor for stockholder-agreement governance provisions, but charter placement remains the safer approach.

contractual_ira_based.location: Investors' Rights Agreement, typically as affirmative or negative covenants.
contractual_ira_based.enforceability: Binding only on the company and investor parties to the IRA. Not binding on future stockholders who don't join the agreement.
typical_provisions: Maintain D&O insurance above specified thresholds
Deliver annual budget and monthly/quarterly financials
Obtain consent before entering related-party transactions
Restrictions on changes to accounting methods
Consent for debt above a specified threshold
contractual_ira_based.trap: If a protective provision is only in the IRA but not in the charter, new investors in later rounds who don't sign the IRA (or sign a separate IRA) are not bound. The company can take the protected action with the new investor's support, circumventing the earlier investors' veto.

protective_provisions_scope.scope_mismatch_trap: The term sheet may list protective provisions as a single block, but in documentation they split between charter (stockholder-level) and IRA (board-level/contractual). Items that appeared to be hard veto rights in the term sheet may end up as softer contractual covenants in the IRA, which are easier to amend or waive.

drag_along.definition: An obligation requiring specified stockholders to vote in favor of and not oppose a sale transaction approved by a defined supermajority of holders.
drag_along.where_defined: Voting Agreement (primary). May also appear in certificate of incorporation post-Moelis.

threshold_variants.standard: Majority of Preferred Stock (as-converted) + majority of Common Stock, each voting as separate classes.
threshold_variants.investor_favorable: Majority of Preferred Stock alone can trigger drag-along.
threshold_variants.founder_favorable: Requires supermajority (66.7%-75%) of all outstanding shares on as-converted basis, plus board approval including founder-designated director.
threshold_variants.trap: If Common Stock approval is not separately required, investors alone can force a sale at a price that returns their liquidation preference but leaves little for common holders.

protections_for_dragged_holders: Same form of consideration as other selling holders (no minority discount)
Representations limited to ownership and authority (no business reps)
Indemnification liability capped at proceeds received
Escrow/holdback obligations pro rata (not disproportionate to minority)

minimum_price_floor.description: Some drag-along provisions include a minimum per-share price below which the drag cannot be triggered.
minimum_price_floor.trap: Without a price floor, investors with a 1x liquidation preference can force a sale at any price that returns their invested capital, even if common holders receive near-zero.

cross_document_consistency_traps.summary: NVCA financing documents are a suite of 5-7 interrelated agreements. Defined terms that appear across multiple documents must be harmonized. The following are the most common and dangerous inconsistency patterns.

major_investor_threshold_mismatch.description: The IRA defines 'Major Investor' for information rights, pro rata rights, and registration rights. The ROFR/Co-Sale Agreement may use the same term or a different threshold for transfer-related rights.
major_investor_threshold_mismatch.failure_mode: An investor qualifies as a Major Investor under the IRA (getting information rights) but not under the ROFR (missing ROFR participation rights), or vice versa. This creates asymmetric rights within the same investor that may not align with deal intent.
major_investor_threshold_mismatch.detection: Compare the defined term in IRA Section 1 with the defined term in ROFR Section 1. Check whether the threshold is dollar-based, share-based, or cross-references the IRA definition. Verify any 'including successors and assigns' language is consistent.
major_investor_threshold_mismatch.fix: ROFR should cross-reference the IRA definition: 'Major Investor shall have the meaning set forth in the Investors' Rights Agreement.' Alternatively, use a single defined term in a master definitions section.

protective_provision_scope_mismatch.description: The term sheet lists protective provisions as a unified block. In documentation, they split between the charter (hard statutory veto) and the IRA (softer contractual covenant). The scope in the charter may be narrower than what was described in the term sheet.
protective_provision_scope_mismatch.failure_mode: Investor believes they have a veto over debt incurrence (listed in term sheet), but that provision ended up only in the IRA as a board-level covenant, not in the charter as a stockholder-level veto. The board can waive it without stockholder consent.
protective_provision_scope_mismatch.detection: Map each term sheet protective provision to its location in the final documents. Verify charter provisions match the term sheet list. Identify any provisions that appear only in the IRA.
protective_provision_scope_mismatch.fix: Create a protective provision mapping table during documentation review. Flag any provision that appears only in the IRA that was listed as a stockholder-level veto in the term sheet.

board_composition_mismatch.description: Board composition is specified in the Voting Agreement (who designates which directors) and must be consistent with the charter (authorized board size and any charter-based designation rights). Post-Moelis, board designation provisions in stockholder agreements without charter support may be unenforceable.
board_composition_mismatch.failure_mode: Voting Agreement grants Series A the right to designate one director, but the charter authorizes a 3-member board with no designation provisions. If challenged, the designation right may be facially invalid under Moelis.
board_composition_mismatch.detection: Compare Voting Agreement board composition provisions with charter authorized board size and any charter-based designation rights. Verify DGCL 122(18) compliance if relying on stockholder agreement provisions.
board_composition_mismatch.fix: Mirror board designation rights in the charter. Ensure the charter authorizes the same board size and composition structure as the Voting Agreement.

conversion_mechanics_mismatch.description: The charter defines conversion mechanics (Conversion Price, anti-dilution adjustments). The SPA may reference a different price or adjustment formula in its representations or conditions.
conversion_mechanics_mismatch.failure_mode: SPA closing condition references 'Conversion Price' but the charter uses 'Applicable Conversion Price' with a different adjustment formula. Creates ambiguity about whether the condition is met.
conversion_mechanics_mismatch.detection: Verify that defined terms in the SPA for pricing and conversion cross-reference the charter definitions, not standalone definitions.
conversion_mechanics_mismatch.fix: SPA should define 'Conversion Price' by reference to the charter: 'as defined in the Restated Certificate.'

fully_diluted_calculation_mismatch.description: Different documents may use different definitions of 'fully diluted' or 'as-converted basis' for ownership calculations, voting thresholds, and pro rata calculations.
fully_diluted_calculation_mismatch.failure_mode: IRA pro rata rights use 'fully diluted' including unissued option pool reserve, but the ROFR participation right uses 'fully diluted' excluding the reserve. An investor's pro rata share differs between the two calculations.
fully_diluted_calculation_mismatch.detection: Search for all uses of 'fully diluted,' 'as-converted,' and 'on a fully-diluted basis' across all documents. Compare whether each reference includes or excludes the same components (options, warrants, convertibles, unissued pool).
fully_diluted_calculation_mismatch.fix: Use a single 'Fully Diluted' definition in one document and cross-reference it from all others. Specify exactly what is included: 'all outstanding shares of Common Stock, all shares of Common Stock issuable upon conversion of all outstanding Preferred Stock, and all shares of Common Stock issuable upon exercise of all outstanding options and warrants, whether vested or unvested, but excluding shares reserved but unissued under the equity incentive plan.'

deemed_liquidation_event_vs_drag_along.description: DLE provisions in the charter and drag-along provisions in the Voting Agreement may apply to the same transaction with different approval thresholds and different economic consequences.
deemed_liquidation_event_vs_drag_along.failure_mode: A merger is approved via drag-along (requiring only majority of Preferred), but the DLE opt-out provision allows holders of a different percentage to elect out of DLE treatment. The transaction closes but some investors opt out of the liquidation waterfall, creating a payment mechanics dispute.
deemed_liquidation_event_vs_drag_along.detection: Compare the drag-along trigger threshold with the DLE opt-out threshold. Verify whether drag-along consent constitutes waiver of DLE opt-out rights.
deemed_liquidation_event_vs_drag_along.fix: Drag-along provision should explicitly state that dragged holders waive their DLE opt-out rights. The Voting Agreement should cross-reference the DLE mechanics.

affiliate_definition_inconsistency.description: 'Affiliate' may be defined differently in the ROFR (for transfer exceptions), the IRA (for competitor carveouts and confidentiality exceptions), and the SPA (for related party transaction representations).
affiliate_definition_inconsistency.failure_mode: Investor transfers shares to a fund affiliate under the ROFR affiliate exception, but the IRA's narrower affiliate definition doesn't cover that entity, meaning the transferee doesn't inherit information rights.
affiliate_definition_inconsistency.detection: Compare the 'Affiliate' definition across all documents. Check whether each definition uses the same control threshold (>50% vs >20%), the same directionality (upstream, downstream, lateral), and the same carveouts (portfolio companies, advisory relationships, LPs).
affiliate_definition_inconsistency.fix: Use a single Affiliate definition and cross-reference it. If different breadths are intended for different contexts (e.g., narrow for competitor exclusions, broad for transfer exceptions), define 'Affiliate' and 'Broad Affiliate' separately and use each deliberately.

ma_exit_considerations.purpose: M&A and exit mechanics affecting venture deal structuring and legal review.
sources: SRS Acquiom 2025 M&A Deal Terms Study (2,200+ transactions 2019-2024)
Harvard Law School Forum on Corporate Governance

priority_order: Senior preferred stock (latest series, unless pari passu)

stacking_vs_pari_passu.stacked: Each series has separate priority. Later series paid first. Default NVCA structure.
stacking_vs_pari_passu.pari_passu: All preferred series treated equally, share pro rata. Occasionally negotiated by early investors.

non_participating_1x.description: Investor chooses EITHER 1x invested capital OR pro-rata common share. Whichever is higher.
non_participating_1x.prevalence: 96-100% of deals across all stages (2024-2025). 97% of non-participating shares carry 1x multiple.
non_participating_1x.conversion_trigger: Investor converts when pro-rata common share exceeds liquidation preference amount.

participating_preferred.description: Investor receives 1x preference PLUS pro-rata share of remaining proceeds (double-dip).
participating_preferred.prevalence: Rare in standard rounds. Appears in distressed/down rounds, bridge financings, and high-risk late-stage deals.

participating_with_cap.description: Participating preferred with maximum total return cap (typically 2x-3x invested capital).
participating_with_cap.notes: Above the cap, investor converts to common. Creates a donut hole where capped participating underperforms uncapped.

higher_multiples.prevalence: Almost exclusively in down rounds, recapitalizations, or distressed bridge financings.
higher_multiples.notes: Higher multiples compound the gap between investor and founder payouts at modest exits.

breakeven_analysis.conversion_point: For 1x non-participating: investor converts when Exit_Value * Ownership_Pct > Investment_Amount.

example.below_breakeven: Investor takes $5M preference; founder gets Exit - $5M
example.above_breakeven: Investor converts, takes 25% of exit; all shareholders pro-rata

breakeven_analysis.founder_retention_payments: In disappointing exits (0.5x-1x capital), acquirers often structure $10M-$50M+ retention packages for founders as employment arrangements, bypassing the liquidation waterfall entirely.

typical_triggers: Merger or consolidation where existing holders lose majority control
Sale of all or substantially all assets
Exclusive licensing of all or substantially all IP
carveouts: Token generation events (must be explicitly carved out in crypto companies)

drag_along.definition: Contractual provision allowing specified majority shareholders to compel all other shareholders to sell on the same terms in an acquisition.
drag_along.location: Typically in Voting Agreement (NVCA model) or Stockholders' Agreement.

nvca_standard.typical_structure: Requires approval of: (1) majority of preferred stock voting as a single class, AND (2) majority of common stock, AND (3) board of directors.
variations: Supermajority (66.7% or 75%) of all outstanding shares
Majority of preferred + majority of common as separate classes
Lead investor consent right layered on top of percentage threshold

trigger_thresholds.range: Thresholds typically 50-75% of outstanding, with 50% + board most common in NVCA-style deals.
trigger_thresholds.trend: Post-Moelis (2024), governance constraints in stockholder agreements are drafted under DGCL 122(18) framework.

price_floor.description: Minimum price per share (often tied to original issue price or a multiple thereof) below which drag-along cannot be exercised.
common_formulations: No drag-along if price per share is below 1x original issue price of most recent preferred round
No drag-along unless all shareholders receive at least their liquidation preference
Floor tied to last 409A valuation or fair market value

consideration_protections: Same form of consideration for all shareholders (cash vs. stock vs. mixed)
Reps and warranties obligations capped at pro-rata share of proceeds
Escrow and holdback obligations shared proportionally
No non-compete obligations imposed on dragged shareholders beyond what principal sellers accept
process_protections: Written notice with minimum response period (typically 10-30 days)
Right to receive same per-share consideration as triggering shareholders
Release of claims limited to those directly related to share ownership

carveouts: Affiliate and related-party transactions (buyer is existing shareholder or affiliate)
Transactions where management retention packages exceed specified percentage of deal value
Transactions involving non-cash consideration without registration rights

tag_along_co_sale.definition: Right of minority shareholders to participate pro-rata in any private sale of shares by major holders, on same terms and price.
tag_along_co_sale.location: NVCA Model Right of First Refusal and Co-Sale Agreement (updated October 2025).

typical_sequence: 1. Selling shareholder receives bona fide third-party offer
2. Selling shareholder delivers transfer notice to company and investors
3. Company has first right to purchase (ROFR) — typically 10-30 days
4. If company declines, investors have secondary ROFR — typically 10-15 additional days
5. If ROFR not fully exercised, remaining investors may exercise co-sale (tag-along) rights
6. If co-sale not exercised, seller may proceed with third-party sale on same or better terms
rofr_interaction.oversubscription: If ROFR demand exceeds available shares, allocated pro rata among exercising parties.
rofr_interaction.lapse_period: Sale must typically close within 60-90 days after ROFR/co-sale period expires, or process restarts.

permitted_transfers_exempt_from_rofr_and: Transfers to family members or family trusts (estate planning)
Transfers to affiliates, subsidiaries, or entities controlled by transferor
Transfers to fellow partners/members of an investor fund
Transfers pursuant to Rule 144 after IPO
Transfers approved by board of directors
Transfers of up to a de minimis amount (varies, often 1-5% of outstanding)
typical_carveouts.conditions_on_permitted_transfers: Transferee must agree to be bound by same ROFR/co-sale agreement. No permitted transfer to competitor of company.

founder_secondary_sale_implications.trigger: Any founder sale of common stock triggers ROFR and co-sale rights unless within carveout.
practical_impact: Founders seeking partial liquidity must navigate ROFR/co-sale process
Board-approved secondary programs (e.g., tender offers) typically structured as carveouts
Company-sponsored tender offers avoid individual ROFR/co-sale triggers
Secondary sale caps (e.g., 10-20% of founder holdings per year) increasingly common
founder_secondary_sale_implications.market_trend: Founder secondary sales increasingly accepted in Series B+ rounds, often with board pre-approval and volume caps.

registration_rights.context: Contractual rights in Investors' Rights Agreement enabling pre-IPO investors to register and resell restricted securities post-IPO.

demand_registration.description: Investors can compel company to file registration statement (Form S-1) to register their shares.

typical_terms.minimum_holders_to_trigger: Holders of 20-67% of registrable securities (varies by deal)
typical_terms.minimum_offering_size: $5M-$25M aggregate offering price to prevent nuisance demands
typical_terms.timing_restriction: Cannot be exercised for 3-7 years post-investment, or 180 days post-IPO
typical_terms.company_deferral_right: Board can defer demand for 60-180 days if disclosure would be materially harmful (typically once per 12-month period)
typical_terms.expense_allocation: Company bears registration expenses (SEC fees, legal, accounting). Selling shareholders bear underwriting discounts and commissions.

s3_registration.description: Shorter-form registration available after company has been public and reporting for 12+ months.
s3_registration.eligibility: Company must have filed all required SEC reports for 12 months and have public float of $75M+ (for shelf registration on Form S-3).

typical_terms.advantage: Significantly cheaper and faster than full S-1 demand registration
typical_terms.shelf_registration: Permits continuous or delayed offerings over 3-year period without new filings

piggyback_registration.description: Right to include shares in any registration the company files for itself or other shareholders.

typical_terms.priority_cutback: Underwriter can cut back piggyback shares. Priority: (1) company shares, (2) demand holders, (3) piggyback holders pro rata.
typical_terms.exceptions: Usually excludes registrations on Form S-4 (M&A) and Form S-8 (employee plans).
typical_terms.no_limit: Typically unlimited number of piggyback exercises

market_standoff_agreement.description: Separate contractual commitment in IRA not to sell for specified period post-IPO, reinforcing underwriter lock-up.
market_standoff_agreement.scope: Covers sales, hedging, short-selling, and derivative transactions
market_standoff_agreement.early_release: Underwriters may grant early release for portion of shares; market standoff typically includes most-favored-nation clause (if any holder released early, all holders released proportionally)

lockup_and_market_standoff.post_lockup_dynamics: Large-scale sell-offs at lock-up expiry can cause 1-3% average stock price decline. Staggered release schedules increasingly used to mitigate.

typical_triggers: When shares become freely tradable under Rule 144 (1-year holding period, adequate current public information)
When holder owns less than 1% of outstanding and can sell all shares in single Rule 144 transaction
Registration statement has been effective for specified period

tail_coverage.definition: Extended reporting period (ERP) endorsement on D&O policy covering claims made after M&A closing for pre-closing acts.
tail_coverage.why_critical: D&O policies are claims-made: coverage depends on when claim is reported, not when act occurred. Without tail, no coverage for post-closing claims about pre-closing conduct.
tail_coverage.typical_duration: 3-6 years. Most common: 6 years (matching typical statute of limitations for breach of fiduciary duty).
tail_coverage.cost: Typically 150-300% of the final annual D&O premium, paid as lump sum at closing.
negotiation_points: Pre-negotiate tail pricing and terms during annual D&O renewal, not at deal closing
Ensure change-of-control definition in policy aligns with deal structure
Verify notice requirements and election deadlines for tail coverage
Confirm tail covers all insured persons, not just named directors
Address straddle claims (arising before closing but reported after)

run_off_policies.definition: Standalone policy purchased at closing specifically to cover pre-closing acts, as alternative to tail endorsement on existing policy.
run_off_policies.vs_tail: Run-off policies offer separate dedicated limits (not shared with acquirer's go-forward program) and can be customized. Usually more expensive but cleaner coverage.
when_preferred: Acquirer wants clean separation from target's historical exposure
Target's existing D&O program has inadequate limits or restrictive terms
Complex multi-entity deals where tail on existing program is insufficient

delaware_framework.dgcl_145: Broad permissive statute allowing corporations to indemnify directors, officers, employees, and agents for litigation expenses and settlements.
delaware_framework.mandatory_vs_permissive: Indemnification for successful defense is mandatory (145(c)). Broader indemnification is permissive and typically granted via charter, bylaws, or separate agreement.
delaware_framework.advancement: Companies may advance expenses before final disposition. If provided in charter/bylaws/agreement, advancement right is contractual and enforceable.

post_merger_survival.key_precedent: OrbiMed v. Symbiomix (Del. Ch. 2024): acquirer assumes target's indemnification obligations. Purchase agreement provisions are 'additional to, not a replacement for' existing rights.
typical_provisions: Merger agreement requires surviving entity to honor pre-closing indemnification agreements for 6 years post-closing
Charter/bylaw provisions protecting directors cannot be amended adversely for 6 years
If surviving entity ceases to exist, obligations transfer to successor
D&O tail coverage requirement as condition to closing
best_practices: Standalone indemnification agreements for each director/officer (not just charter/bylaw reliance)
Explicit survival language in merger agreement
Tail D&O insurance as backstop to contractual indemnification
Escrow or letter of credit to secure indemnification obligations if acquirer creditworthiness is concern

rwi_insurance.definition: Representations and Warranties Insurance (RWI) covers losses from breaches of seller reps and warranties in M&A purchase agreements.

prevalence.overall: Used in ~75% of PE transactions and ~64% of larger strategic acquisitions (2024-2025).
prevalence.vc_exits: Increasingly common in VC exits above $50M deal value. Less prevalent in sub-$30M transactions due to minimum premium economics.
prevalence.trend: Adoption accelerated over past 5 years as premiums decreased and familiarity increased.

economics.premiums: 2.5%-3.5% of policy limit (down from 3-4% in prior years)
economics.retention_deductible: Typically 1% of deal value (first-dollar loss borne by insured)

escrow_reduction.without_rwi: Escrow typically 5-15% of purchase price held for 12-24 months
escrow_reduction.with_rwi: Escrow reduced to 0.5-1% (or eliminated entirely for retention/deductible only)
escrow_reduction.benefit: Sellers receive more cash at closing; particularly valuable for VC fund distributions

indemnification_changes.survival_periods: Shortened when RWI in place (12-15 months vs 18-24 months without)
indemnification_changes.caps: Seller indemnification cap reduced to escrow amount (or zero in full RWI deals)
indemnification_changes.baskets: Deductible baskets may increase when RWI backstops the risk

impact_on_deal_structure.competitive_advantage: Buyers offering RWI-backed terms differentiate in competitive auction processes by being more seller-friendly.

common: Known breaches or matters identified in due diligence
Purchase price adjustments and working capital disputes
Environmental liabilities (often require separate environmental policy)
Transfer pricing and tax positions (partially available via tax opinion add-on)

vc_specific_considerations: Fund distribution timing: RWI enables faster LP distributions by reducing escrow holdbacks
Multiple seller complexity: avoids joint-and-several indemnification among many small shareholders
Founder departing sellers: reduces post-closing tension between founders and acquirer
Clean exit for fund managers: no contingent liability reporting to LPs after fund wind-down

earnout_structures.definition: Contingent consideration in M&A where portion of purchase price is paid based on target achieving post-closing performance milestones.

market_prevalence.overall: 22% of all M&A deals in 2024 (excluding life sciences). Rising due to valuation gaps.
market_prevalence.life_sciences: Significantly higher prevalence (40-60% of deals) due to regulatory milestone uncertainty.
market_prevalence.driver: Average transaction multiples declined from 11.9x (2016-2021) to 9.8x (2022-2024), making earnouts necessary to bridge valuation gaps.
market_prevalence.sector_expansion: Earnouts now appear in >25% of industrial and IT deals, well beyond traditional life sciences.

financial_metric_based.prevalence: Most common outside life sciences. EBITDA most frequently used.
financial_metric_based.measurement_periods: Trending shorter: many now <2 years (previously 3-year standard).

milestone_based.prevalence: Dominant in life sciences and technology acquisitions.
milestone_based.key_issue: Buyer obligation to pursue milestones — 'commercially reasonable efforts' standard is heavily litigated.

time_based.description: Payment contingent on continued employment or retention of key personnel.
time_based.tax_treatment: IRS may recharacterize as compensation rather than purchase price, affecting tax treatment for both parties.
time_based.prevalence: Less common as standalone; often layered with financial or milestone metrics.

hybrid.description: Combines financial metrics with milestones or time-based conditions.
hybrid.trend: Increasingly complex structures reflecting sophisticated risk allocation.

liability_classified.description: Remeasured to fair value each reporting period until resolved.
liability_classified.impact: Creates P&L volatility as earnout fair value changes.

equity_classified.description: No remeasurement after initial recognition. Fixed at closing.
equity_classified.requirements: Must qualify as fixed-for-fixed arrangement or phantom equity.
equity_classified.advantage: Eliminates earnings volatility from contingent consideration.
equity_classified.trend: Growing adoption in PE-backed deals to reduce financial statement noise.

tax_considerations.seller: Earnout payments generally capital gain if structured as purchase price; ordinary income if recharacterized as compensation.
tax_considerations.buyer: Earnout payments may be deductible if characterized as compensation; added to basis if purchase price.
tax_considerations.installment_sale: Sellers may use installment method (IRC 453) to defer gain recognition on contingent payments.

dispute_landscape.frequency: Post-closing earnout disputes are mounting (2024-2025). Delaware courts seeing increased litigation.
common_disputes: Buyer manipulation of post-closing operations to avoid earnout targets
Disagreement over 'commercially reasonable efforts' to achieve milestones
Accounting methodology disputes (GAAP vs. non-GAAP, one-time adjustments)
Integration decisions that undermine standalone earnout performance
delaware_2024_guidance: Clear, unambiguous contract language is paramount — courts will enforce plain meaning
Independent expert dispute resolution procedures (accounting firm arbitration) reduce litigation
Documented commercial rationale for buyer decisions affecting earnout metrics provides protection
Implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing applies even absent express efforts covenants
risk_mitigation: Detailed definitions of all financial metrics with explicit adjustment methodologies
Separate P&L or business unit tracking for earnout measurement
Negative covenants restricting buyer actions that could undermine earnout achievement
Third-party accounting firm arbitration for disputed calculations
Acceleration provisions if buyer materially changes target's business

de_spac_process.overview: Merger between SPAC (publicly listed blank-check company) and private target, resulting in target becoming publicly traded without traditional IPO.
key_steps: 1. SPAC identifies target and negotiates definitive merger agreement
2. SEC review of proxy statement / registration statement (S-4 or F-4)
3. SPAC shareholders vote on merger; concurrent redemption election period
4. PIPE or other committed financing closes concurrently
5. Merger closes; target company begins trading under new ticker
6. Post-closing SEC reporting obligations commence immediately
de_spac_process.timeline: Typically 4-6 months from LOI to closing. SEC review is primary timeline driver.

sec_2024_final_rules.enhanced_disclosures: Expanded disclosure requirements for projections, conflicts of interest, dilution, and sponsor economics.
sec_2024_final_rules.liability_alignment: Target company treated as co-registrant, aligning liability standards with traditional IPOs.
sec_2024_final_rules.projections: Enhanced requirements for disclosure of assumptions and bases for forward-looking statements.

pipe_financing.purpose: Backstop against shareholder redemptions and provide minimum cash to complete merger.
pipe_financing.typical_size: $50M-$500M+ depending on deal size and expected redemptions.
structures_2024_2025: Non-redemption agreements (NRAs) — investor agrees not to redeem in exchange for additional shares
pipe_financing.trend: Traditional PIPE has declined as redemption rates increased. FPAs and NRAs gaining share. 'Share + rights' model replacing 'share + warrant' structures.

redemption_mechanics.right: SPAC IPO shareholders can redeem shares for pro-rata trust value (~$10/share plus interest) regardless of merger vote.
redemption_mechanics.current_rates: Redemption rates frequently exceed 80-90% in 2024-2025, leaving minimal trust cash for combined company.
redemption_mechanics.impact: High redemptions create cash shortfalls, requiring larger PIPEs, FPAs, or alternative financing.
redemption_mechanics.deadline_structure: New SPACs: 12-18 month initial period with 6-month extensions. Older vintage SPACs face liquidation deadlines.

current_market_2024_2025.volume: SPACs accounted for 58-65% of US IPO volume by mid-2025. 92 SPAC IPOs raising $11.7B through Q3 2025.
current_market_2024_2025.label: Market termed 'SPAC 2.0' or 'SPAC 4.0' reflecting structural reforms and improved discipline.
current_market_2024_2025.sponsor_quality: Narrower, more experienced sponsor base. Fewer first-time sponsors.
current_market_2024_2025.target_requirements: Revenue thresholds typically $50M+ annual revenue for de-SPAC targets.
current_market_2024_2025.performance_improvements: Success rates improved to 40-50% with performance-based sponsor incentives.
challenges: Compressed IPO market driving demand but also increasing competition

direct_listings.definition: Company lists existing shares directly on exchange without underwriters, IPO pricing, or new capital raise (unless using primary direct listing rules).

primary_direct_listing.description: SEC-approved rules (NYSE 2020, Nasdaq 2021) allow companies to raise primary capital in direct listings.
nasdaq_requirements: Unrestricted publicly held shares + new shares market value >= $110M (or $100M with $110M stockholders' equity)
50% of round lot holders with >= $2,500 market value in unrestricted securities
primary_direct_listing.pricing_flexibility: 2022 SEC-approved changes: transactions can proceed at opening auction prices within band of 20% below to 80% above registration range.

advantages: No underwriter fees (saving 3-7% of offering size)
No lock-up period (existing shareholders can sell immediately)
No share dilution from new issuance (in secondary-only listings)
Price discovery via open market auction rather than bookbuilding
Timeline: ~90 days vs 12+ months for traditional IPO
disadvantages: No underwriter price support or stabilization
No guaranteed capital raise (in secondary-only listings)
Higher price volatility on first trading day
Limited analyst coverage and investor education pre-listing
Requires existing strong brand recognition and investor interest
vc_exit_implications: No lock-up provides immediate liquidity for VC investors
No underwriter allocation — all sales at market price
Best suited for well-known companies with existing secondary market trading

dual_class_structures.definition: Share structure with two or more classes carrying different voting rights, typically Class A (1 vote/share, public) and Class B (10 votes/share, founders/insiders).
dual_class_structures.prevalence: 25-33% of US IPOs in 2024-2025, up from <5% in mid-1990s. Concentrated in technology sector.

typical_structure.class_a: 1 vote per share. Issued to public investors.
typical_structure.class_b: 10 votes per share (range: 2-20x). Held by founders and early insiders.
typical_structure.economic_rights: Identical economic rights (dividends, liquidation) across classes.
typical_structure.control_implication: Founder with 15% economic ownership can hold >60% voting power.

governance_implications: Founders maintain control despite minority economic ownership
Protection against hostile takeovers and activist campaigns
Reduced accountability to public shareholders on operational decisions
Index inclusion challenges (S&P 500 no longer admits new dual-class; FTSE Russell requires minimum 5% voting in public hands)
Institutional investor opposition (ISS, Glass Lewis recommend voting against dual-class)

sunset_provisions.definition: Mechanisms that automatically convert dual-class to single-class (one share, one vote) upon specified triggers.

time_based.description: Automatic conversion after fixed period (typically 7-10 years post-IPO).
time_based.prevalence: Most common sunset type. Median duration ~10 years.

ownership_based.description: Conversion when founder/insider ownership falls below threshold (typically 5-15% of outstanding).
ownership_based.purpose: Ensures control ends when insider economic interest becomes de minimis.

event_based.description: Conversion on death, disability, or departure of specified founder(s).
event_based.purpose: Ties enhanced voting to specific individual's involvement.

transfer_based.description: High-vote shares automatically convert to low-vote upon transfer to non-permitted holder.
transfer_based.prevalence: Nearly universal — prevents control transfer without economic transfer.

extension_controversies.issue: As sunsets approach, companies seek extensions. Raises fiduciary duty and entrenchment concerns.
extension_controversies.key_litigation: Trade Desk case addressed whether charter should pre-specify extension conditions at IPO.
extension_controversies.trend: Proposals for contractual approaches that improve shareholder information about extension terms at IPO stage.

sunset_provisions.empirical_evidence: Dual-class CEO median post-IPO tenure: 6.6 years vs 4.3 years for single-class. Most CEO turnovers occur before sunset triggers, driven by performance rather than governance mechanisms.

vc_investor_considerations: Pre-IPO negotiation of sunset terms is critical — harder to change post-IPO
Dual-class may reduce post-IPO acquisition premium (buyer must negotiate with controlling founder)
Impact on fund's ability to exit via secondary market sale vs. M&A
Some LPs have policies restricting investment in dual-class companies post-IPO

securities_law.description: Federal and state securities law fundamentals for private venture capital placements.

regulation_d.overview: Regulation D provides exemptions from SEC registration for private securities offerings. Currently comprises Rules 504, 506(b), and 506(c). Rule 505 was repealed effective May 22, 2017.

rule_504.general_solicitation: Permitted under certain conditions (state registration, accredited-only, or state-regulated intermediary)
rule_504.investor_limits: No limit on accredited or non-accredited investors
rule_504.disclosure_requirements: No specific federal disclosure requirements for non-accredited investors
rule_504.securities_type: May be unrestricted if sold under state registration or accredited-only exemption; otherwise restricted
excluded_issuers: Companies with no specific business plan or M&A-only purpose
rule_504.notes: Amended in 2016 when Rule 505 was repealed; offering limit raised from $1M to $5M, then to $10M.

rule_505.reason: Eliminated as redundant after Rule 504 amendments raised its offering limit and added bad actor disqualification.

rule_506b.general_solicitation: Prohibited. No general solicitation or general advertising permitted.

non_accredited_investors.sophistication_requirement: Each non-accredited investor (alone or with purchaser representative) must have sufficient knowledge and experience in financial and business matters to evaluate the merits and risks of the investment
non_accredited_investors.disclosure_if_present: If any non-accredited investors participate, issuer must provide disclosure documents comparable to Regulation A, including financial statements (audited if Series A or later)

rule_506b.verification: Self-certification accepted. No requirement to take 'reasonable steps' to verify accredited status; issuer may rely on investor representations.
rule_506b.pre_existing_relationship: Required for all offerees (substantive, pre-existing relationship with issuer or its agents)
rule_506b.securities_type: Restricted securities — cannot be freely resold
rule_506b.notes: Most commonly used exemption for VC financings. ~90% of Reg D capital raised under 506(b).

rule_506c.statutory_basis: JOBS Act Section 201, implemented by SEC Release 33-9415
rule_506c.general_solicitation: Permitted. General solicitation and advertising allowed (websites, media, public events).
rule_506c.accredited_investors: All purchasers must be accredited investors. Zero non-accredited investors permitted.

verification.standard: Issuer must take 'reasonable steps' to verify each purchaser's accredited investor status
factors_considered: Nature of purchaser and type of accredited investor category claimed
Amount and type of information about the purchaser available to the issuer
Nature of the offering (manner of solicitation, minimum investment amount)
Terms of the offering affecting risk of non-compliance
safe_harbor_methods_for_income: Review IRS forms (W-2, 1099, K-1, 1040) for two most recent years plus written representation of current-year expectation
Written confirmation from registered broker-dealer, SEC-registered investment adviser, licensed CPA, or licensed attorney
safe_harbor_methods_for_net_worth: Review bank/brokerage/securities statements, CDs, tax assessments, appraisal reports from prior 3 months plus written representation of all liabilities
Credit report check within prior 3 months combined with asset verification
Written confirmation from registered broker-dealer, SEC-registered investment adviser, licensed CPA, or licensed attorney
verification.third_party_verification: Third-party verification services (e.g., VerifyInvestor, Accredify) accepted

march_2025_guidance.description: SEC no-action letter (March 12, 2025) significantly eased verification burdens
march_2025_guidance.permitted_approach: Issuers may rely on minimum investment amounts as a reasonable verification step combined with: (1) written investor representations of accredited status, (2) representations that investment is not third-party financed, and (3) issuer confirmation of no actual knowledge of non-accreditation
march_2025_guidance.impact: Reduces administrative burden that previously discouraged 506(c) usage

rule_506c.notes: Usage increasing after March 2025 guidance. Previously underutilized due to verification burden.

form_d_filing.deadline: Within 15 calendar days after the first sale of securities in the offering
form_d_filing.deadline_extension: If 15-day period ends on weekend or holiday, due date extends to next business day

amendment_requirements.annual: On or before first anniversary of most recent filing if offering is continuing
amendment_requirements.material_changes: As soon as practicable after any material change in information
amendment_requirements.errors: As soon as practicable after discovery of material mistake of fact or error

penalties_for_late_filing.additional_risk: Potential prohibition from future reliance on Regulation D exemptions without SEC waiver
penalties_for_late_filing.first_enforcement: December 2024 — SEC settled first enforcement actions solely for Rule 503 (Form D) violations

form_d_filing.is_condition_of_exemption: Filing failure does not automatically destroy the exemption, but SEC may bring enforcement action and state regulators may deny state exemption

nsmia_blue_sky_preemption.statute: National Securities Markets Improvement Act of 1996 (NSMIA), codified at Securities Act Section 18
nsmia_blue_sky_preemption.effect: Preempts state registration/qualification requirements for 'covered securities'
covered_securities_include: Securities listed on national exchanges (NYSE, NASDAQ)
Securities issued by registered investment companies
Securities offered to 'qualified purchasers' as SEC-defined
not_preempted: Rule 504 offerings (subject to full state blue sky compliance)
state_retained_powers: Notice filing requirements and fees for covered securities
Investigation and enforcement of fraud claims
nsmia_blue_sky_preemption.practical_impact: Rule 506 issuers avoid state merit review but must still file state notices and pay fees in each state where securities are sold

income_test.individual: $200,000 annual income in each of the two most recent years with reasonable expectation of same in current year
income_test.joint_with_spouse_or_partner: $300,000 combined annual income in each of the two most recent years with reasonable expectation of same in current year
income_test.spousal_equivalent: Added by 2020 amendments — domestic partner who shares joint financial resources

net_worth_test.threshold: $1,000,000 net worth (individually or jointly with spouse/partner)
net_worth_test.primary_residence_exclusion: Primary residence excluded from assets. Mortgage on primary residence excluded from liabilities UNLESS underwater (excess of mortgage over fair market value counts as liability).

natural_person_thresholds.notes_on_thresholds: Thresholds have not been adjusted for inflation since originally set in 1982. Various proposals to index have not been adopted.

professional_certification_pathway.future_additions: SEC may designate additional certifications (CFA and CAIA under consideration)

knowledgeable_employees.definition: Employees of private fund investment managers who participate in investment activities of the fund
knowledgeable_employees.basis: Rule 3c-5 under the Investment Company Act
includes: Executive officers, directors, trustees, general partners, advisory board members of the investment manager or any affiliate managing the fund's investments
Employees who have participated in the investment activities of the private fund or other private funds managed by the same manager for at least 12 months
knowledgeable_employees.does_not_include: Clerical, administrative, or support staff who do not participate in investment decisions

financial_institutions.threshold: No minimum asset test for banks, S&Ls, insurance companies, registered investment companies, BDCs, SBICs
financial_institutions.condition: Acting for own account or accounts of other accredited investors

general_entities.condition: Not formed for the specific purpose of acquiring the securities offered

family_offices.additional: Family clients of qualifying family offices also qualify

entity_owned_by_accredited_investors.rule: Any entity in which all equity owners are individually accredited investors

verification_requirements_by_exemption.rule_506b: Self-certification accepted. Issuer may reasonably rely on investor questionnaire or written representation. No documentation review required.
verification_requirements_by_exemption.rule_506c: Issuer must take 'reasonable steps' to verify. Documentation review or third-party verification required. See regulation_d.rule_506c.verification for methods.
verification_requirements_by_exemption.regulation_cf: Self-certification; platform performs investment limit checks

accredited_investor.population_estimate: Approximately 24.3 million US households (18.5%) meet financial thresholds as of 2024 SEC estimates

covered_persons: The issuer, including predecessors and affiliated issuers
Directors, executive officers, and other officers of the issuer participating in the offering
General partners and managing members of the issuer
Beneficial owners of 20% or more of the issuer's outstanding voting equity securities (calculated on total voting power basis)
Promoters connected with the issuer at the time of sale
Investment managers of issuer (if pooled investment fund) and directors, executive officers, and other officers of the investment manager participating in the offering
General partners and managing members of the investment manager
Directors, executive officers, and other officers of any compensated solicitor participating in the offering

disqualifying_events.scope: Felony or misdemeanor in connection with purchase or sale of any security, making any false filing with SEC, or conduct of underwriter/broker/dealer/investment adviser
disqualifying_events.lookback: 10 years (5 years if issuer or affiliate)

disqualifying_events.scope: Final order of court of competent jurisdiction restraining person from engaging in or continuing securities-related conduct

disqualifying_events.event: Final order from state/federal regulatory authority
disqualifying_events.scope: Final order of state securities, insurance, banking, savings, or credit union regulator; federal banking agency; CFTC; or the NCUA that bars person from association with regulated entity or from engaging in securities, insurance, or banking business, or is based on fraud/deceit/manipulation

SEC order suspending or revoking registration as broker-dealer, investment adviser, or investment company, or imposing cease-and-desist order for violation of scienter-based anti-fraud provisions or Section 5 of Securities Act
disqualifying_events.lookback: No time limit for revocations; varies for other orders

disqualifying_events.event: SEC stop order or refusal order
disqualifying_events.scope: Order suspending Regulation A exemption or denying/suspending effectiveness of registration statement
Suspension or expulsion from membership in, or association with, an SRO (e.g., FINRA)

disqualifying_events.event: US Postal Service false representation order
disqualifying_events.scope: Final order based on violation of postal fraud statutes

pre_effective_date_events.rule: Events before September 23, 2013 do not trigger disqualification but MUST be disclosed to investors under Rule 506(e)
pre_effective_date_events.disclosure_timing: A reasonable time prior to sale
pre_effective_date_events.disclosure_method: Written disclosure sufficient; no prescribed format

reasonable_care_defense.rule: Rule 506(d)(2)(iv) — disqualification does not apply if issuer can establish it did not know, and in exercise of reasonable care could not have known, that a covered person had a disqualifying event
reasonable_care_includes: Factual inquiry into whether any covered person has a disqualifying event
Questionnaires, certifications, or contractual representations from covered persons
Checking public databases (SEC EDGAR, FINRA BrokerCheck, state regulatory databases)
Representations from compensated solicitors regarding their covered persons
reasonable_care_defense.continuous_offerings: For ongoing or long-lived offerings, periodic updates required — bring-down of representations, re-checking databases, annual re-certification
reasonable_care_defense.burden_of_proof: On the issuer to demonstrate reasonable care was exercised

waivers.authority: SEC Division of Corporation Finance grants waivers upon showing of good cause
waivers.standard: Higher burden for criminal convictions and scienter-based violations involving securities sales
waivers.request_process: Written request to SEC with supporting factual basis
waivers.notes: Waivers are matter of SEC discretion; no automatic right

general_principle.rule: Rule 152(a): Offerings will not be integrated if, based on particular facts and circumstances, the issuer can establish that each offering either (1) complies with Securities Act registration requirements, or (2) qualifies for an available exemption from registration
general_principle.for_exempt_offerings_prohibiting_general: Issuer must have a reasonable belief that each purchaser in the offering prohibiting general solicitation was either: (A) not solicited through general solicitation, or (B) established a substantive relationship with the issuer prior to commencement of the offering prohibiting general solicitation

safe_harbors.description: Any offering made more than 30 calendar days before the commencement of any other offering, or more than 30 calendar days after the termination or completion of any other offering, will not be integrated with such other offering
safe_harbors.condition: For offerings prohibiting general solicitation: issuer must have a reasonable belief that no purchaser was solicited through general solicitation in the earlier offering
safe_harbors.notes: Replaced the prior six-month safe harbor. Significantly shortened the cooling-off period.

safe_harbors.description: Offers and sales made in compliance with Rule 701, pursuant to employee benefit plans, or in compliance with Regulation S will not be integrated with other offerings

An offering for which a registration statement has been filed will not be integrated with a terminated or completed offering under an exemption, provided the exempted offering: (A) prohibited general solicitation, (B) was limited to QIBs and institutional accredited investors under Rule 163B, or (C) was made under Regulation S
Offers and sales made in reliance on an exemption permitting general solicitation will not be integrated with another terminated or completed offering

former_six_month_rule.status: Superseded by Rule 152 but still relevant as analytical reference
former_six_month_rule.old_rule: Under former Rule 502(a), offerings separated by more than six months were presumed not integrated
former_six_month_rule.current_analog: Rule 152(b)(1) 30-day safe harbor replaced this, substantially reducing the required separation period
former_six_month_rule.caution: Some practitioners still use six-month separation as conservative best practice

practical_vc_concerns: Bridge financing followed by priced round: if bridge uses general solicitation, integration risk with subsequent 506(b) offering
SAFE followed by priced equity: generally not integrated if separated by >30 days or if both rely on same exemption type
Concurrent offerings in different jurisdictions: Regulation S offshore offering generally safe-harbored from domestic offering
Rolling closes: multiple closes under same offering generally treated as single offering, not integrated separate offerings

clarifications: Merely having an effective registration statement does not automatically raise integration concerns with concurrent private placements
Issuer can include securities from a subsequent private placement in a pre-effective amendment to a pending resale registration statement if the placement complies with Rule 152

rule_144.purpose: Provides safe harbor for resale of restricted securities and control securities without registration

holding_period.reporting_companies: 6 months from date full purchase price paid
holding_period.non_reporting_companies: 1 year from date full purchase price paid
holding_period.measurement: Begins when purchaser pays full consideration, not the issuance date
holding_period.tacking: Permitted for securities acquired through stock splits, dividends, conversions, or gifts from same holder

current_public_information.reporting_companies: Issuer must be current in SEC reporting obligations (10-K, 10-Q, 8-K filings)
current_public_information.non_reporting_companies: Certain basic information about the issuer must be publicly available (incorporated jurisdiction, nature of business, identity of officers/directors, most recent balance sheet, most recent P&L)
current_public_information.after_one_year_non_affiliates: Non-affiliates may sell after 1 year even without current public information for reporting companies

volume_limitations.applies_to: Affiliates only (not non-affiliates after holding period)
volume_limitations.equity_securities: Greater of: (1) 1% of outstanding shares of same class, or (2) average weekly trading volume during four calendar weeks preceding Form 144 filing
volume_limitations.debt_securities: Greater of: (1) 10% of tranche, or (2) 10% of principal amount outstanding

requirements: Must be sold in brokers' transactions (Section 4(a)(4))
manner_of_sale.prohibited: Solicitation of buy orders by seller or anyone acting on seller's behalf (beyond normal broker inquiry)

form_144_filing.applies_to: Affiliates selling more than 5,000 shares or $50,000 in any 3-month period
form_144_filing.timing: Must be filed concurrently with placing the sell order with the broker
