form_144_filing.filed_with: SEC via EDGAR (electronic filing required since April 2023)
contents: Issuer name and SEC file number
Seller's name and relationship to issuer
Title and number of shares to be sold
form_144_filing.validity: Sale must be executed within 90 days of filing; otherwise new Form 144 required

non_affiliate_simplified_path.after_6_months_reporting: May sell freely subject only to current public information requirement (no volume, manner of sale, or Form 144 requirements)
non_affiliate_simplified_path.after_1_year_reporting: May sell freely with no conditions whatsoever
non_affiliate_simplified_path.after_1_year_non_reporting: May sell freely with no conditions

rule_144.shell_company_restrictions: Rule 144 unavailable for securities of current or former shell companies unless: (1) 12 months elapsed since Form 10 information filed, (2) issuer has ceased to be a shell company, and (3) issuer is current in reporting at time of sale

rule_144a.purpose: Safe harbor for resale of restricted securities to qualified institutional buyers (QIBs) without registration

qib_definition.general_threshold: Entity that owns and invests on discretionary basis at least $100 million in securities of non-affiliated issuers
qib_definition.registered_dealers: $10 million in securities of non-affiliated issuers
qib_definition.banks_and_s_and_ls: $100 million in non-affiliated securities AND at least $25 million audited net worth
qualifying_entity_types: Trust funds with aggregate assets of at least $100M
Any entity owned entirely by QIBs

conditions: Sale must be to a QIB or to a person the seller reasonably believes is a QIB
Seller must take reasonable steps to ensure buyer is aware seller relies on Rule 144A exemption
Securities must not be of same class as securities listed on a national securities exchange
Buyer has right to request issuer information (financial statements, business description)
key_characteristics: Securities remain restricted after purchase — subsequent resale must also comply with Rule 144A or another exemption
QIBs may re-sell to other QIBs under Rule 144A (daisy-chain permitted)
rule_144a.vc_relevance: Primarily used in debt and PIPE transactions. Less common in early-stage VC but relevant for secondary sales of late-stage private company shares to institutional buyers.

anti_fraud_provisions.overview: Federal anti-fraud provisions apply to ALL securities transactions regardless of registration or exemption status. No exemption from anti-fraud liability exists for private placements.

rule_10b5.governing_rule: 17 CFR 240.10b-5, promulgated under Section 10(b) of Securities Exchange Act of 1934
rule_10b5.scope: Purchase OR sale of any security (broader than Section 17(a) which covers offers and sales only)

three_prongs.10b5_a: Prohibits employing any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud
three_prongs.10b5_b: Prohibits making any untrue statement of a material fact or omitting to state a material fact necessary to make statements not misleading
three_prongs.10b5_c: Prohibits engaging in any act, practice, or course of business that operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person

elements_of_private_action: Scienter (intent to deceive, manipulate, or defraud)
Connection with purchase or sale of a security
Reliance (actual or presumed via fraud-on-the-market doctrine)

scienter_requirement.standard: Knowing or intentional misconduct; recklessness sufficient in most circuits

key_cases.ernst_v_hochfelder_1976: Supreme Court held scienter required; negligence insufficient under Section 10(b)
key_cases.tellabs_v_makor_2007: Scienter inference must be 'cogent and at least as compelling' as any opposing inference of nonfraudulent intent

enforcement.sec_enforcement: Civil actions for injunctions, disgorgement, civil penalties
enforcement.private_right_of_action: Implied private right of action recognized by courts (not explicit in statute)
enforcement.criminal_enforcement: DOJ may bring criminal charges under Section 32 of Exchange Act (willful violations)

section_17a.governing_statute: Section 17(a) of the Securities Act of 1933
section_17a.scope: Offer or sale of securities (narrower than 10b-5 — does not cover purchases)

17a_1.text: Prohibits employing any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud

17a_2.text: Prohibits obtaining money or property by means of any untrue statement of a material fact or omission

17a_3.text: Prohibits engaging in any transaction, practice, or course of business that operates as a fraud or deceit

section_17a.key_case: Aaron v. SEC (1980) — Supreme Court held 17(a)(1) requires scienter, but 17(a)(2) and 17(a)(3) do not
section_17a.private_right_of_action: None. Only SEC can enforce Section 17(a). Circuit split historically, but majority view (and SEC position) is no private right.
section_17a.significance_for_vc: SEC can bring fraud claims against VC term sheet misrepresentations under 17(a)(2) and (a)(3) based on negligence alone — no need to prove intent to deceive

materiality_standard.primary_test: Information is material if there is a substantial likelihood that a reasonable investor would consider it important in making an investment decision — i.e., it would significantly alter the 'total mix' of information available

key_cases.tsc_industries_v_northway_1976: Established the 'total mix' standard for materiality under proxy rules; adopted broadly for all securities fraud
key_cases.basic_inc_v_levinson_1988: Extended materiality test to forward-looking statements (merger negotiations); adopted probability-magnitude test for contingent/speculative events
key_cases.matrixx_v_siracusano_2011: Reaffirmed that statistical significance is not required for materiality; probability-magnitude test applies

materiality_standard.probability_magnitude_test: For contingent or speculative events, materiality determined by balancing: (1) the probability that the event will occur, and (2) the anticipated magnitude of the event in light of totality of company activity
materiality_standard.no_bright_line: Materiality is inherently fact-specific; no bright-line quantitative thresholds

application_to_vc_term_sheets: Representations about company financials, revenue, customer counts — actionable if materially false or misleading
Omission of material liabilities, pending litigation, or regulatory issues
Forward-looking projections: protected by PSLRA safe harbor for public companies but NO safe harbor for private placements
Cap table representations: material misstatements about outstanding shares, options, SAFEs, or conversion mechanics
IP ownership representations: failure to disclose known IP defects or pending claims
Use of proceeds representations: diversion from stated use may constitute fraud
Anti-fraud applies even to exempt private placements — no exemption from 10b-5 or 17(a)

hsr_act.governing_statute: Hart-Scott-Rodino Antitrust Improvements Act of 1976 (15 U.S.C. § 18a)
hsr_act.enforced_by: Federal Trade Commission (FTC) and Department of Justice Antitrust Division

size_of_person_tests.smaller_person: $26.8 million in total assets or annual net sales
size_of_person_tests.larger_person: $267.8 million in total assets or annual net sales

current_thresholds_2026.no_size_of_person_threshold: $535.5 million (if transaction exceeds this, filing required regardless of party sizes)
current_thresholds_2026.adjustment_mechanism: Adjusted annually based on change in gross national product per 15 U.S.C. § 18a(a)(2)

notification_requirements.who_files: Both acquiring person and acquired person (or acquired entity) must file
notification_requirements.filing_contents: HSR Form with information about parties, transaction structure, market overlaps, prior acquisitions, and revenue data by NAICS codes
notification_requirements.new_form_requirements_2025: Expanded HSR form effective February 2025 requires substantially more information including transaction rationale, overlapping products/services, labor market information, and prior acquisitions

waiting_periods.initial_waiting_period: 30 calendar days from date both filings received by agencies (15 days for cash tender offers and bankruptcy acquisitions)
waiting_periods.early_termination: Agencies may grant early termination of waiting period (suspended since February 2021, reinstatement uncertain)
waiting_periods.second_request: If agency issues a Request for Additional Information ('Second Request'), new waiting period of 30 days begins after substantial compliance with the request
waiting_periods.second_request_timeline: Compliance with Second Requests typically takes 3-12 months

investment_only_exemption.rule: Acquisition of voting securities solely for the purpose of investment where acquiring person holds 10% or less of issuer's outstanding voting securities
investment_only_exemption.standard: Acquirer must have NO intent to participate in formulation, determination, or direction of issuer's basic business decisions
inconsistent_conduct: Proposing corporate actions requiring shareholder vote
Being a competitor of the issuer
Sending letters to management about strategy or transaction structure
investment_only_exemption.vc_problem: Most VC investors intend to take board seats or influence governance, making this exemption unavailable for typical VC investments
investment_only_exemption.penalties: Civil penalties of $51,744 per day (2025 rate) for failure to file when required

acquisitions_of_non_voting_securities.rule: HSR applies only to acquisitions of 'voting securities,' assets, or non-corporate interests (LLC/LP). Acquisition of non-voting preferred stock or debt instruments is generally exempt.
acquisitions_of_non_voting_securities.convertible_securities: Convertible voting securities acquired solely for investment purposes may be exempt if acquirer does not currently hold an option or right to convert and has no present intent to convert

other_common_exemptions: Acquisitions of goods or realty in the ordinary course of business
Acquisitions of bonds, mortgages, deeds of trust, or other obligations that are not voting securities
Acquisitions of small percentages of an issuer's voting securities (if below the minimum notification threshold)
Foreign transactions meeting the exemption criteria

vc_practical_notes: Most preferred stock in VC rounds is voting — HSR analysis required if thresholds met
Series-on-series aggregation: all voting securities of the same issuer held by acquirer and its associates are aggregated
Fund-level vs. management company aggregation: generally funds under common management are aggregated as one 'person'
GP/LP structure: acquiring 'person' is typically the fund (LP), not the GP — but controlled entities of GP may be associated persons

state_securities_law.overview: State securities laws ('blue sky laws') operate alongside federal law. NSMIA preempts state registration for Rule 506 offerings but states retain anti-fraud authority and notice filing requirements.

california.governing_law: California Corporate Securities Law of 1968 (Cal. Corp. Code §§ 25000-25707)
california.regulator: Department of Financial Protection and Innovation (DFPI)

section_25102f_exemption.name: Limited Offering Exemption

requirements.purchaser_limit: No more than 35 purchasers (including persons outside California)
excluded_from_count: Officers, directors, and managers of issuer
Employees and affiliates under employee benefit plans
requirements.purchaser_qualification: Each purchaser must either: (A) have a preexisting personal or business relationship with offeror, its officers, directors, or controlling persons, OR (B) have sufficient business/financial experience (personally or through unaffiliated professional adviser) to evaluate the merits and risks
requirements.investment_intent: Each purchaser must represent in writing that they are purchasing for own account (or trust account) and not for resale or distribution
requirements.no_advertising: No general advertising or general solicitation permitted
requirements.no_commissions: No commissions or similar compensation paid for solicitation (with exceptions for licensed broker-dealers)

notice_filing.deadline: Within 15 calendar days after first sale in California
notice_filing.late_filing_cure: If issuer fails to file timely, must file within 15 business days of demand and pay fee equal to standard qualification fee

section_25102f_exemption.failure_to_file_effect: Failure to file notice does NOT destroy the exemption — exemption is self-executing. But issuer may face enforcement action.

california.qualification_requirement: Securities must be 'qualified' (California's term for registered) with the DFPI unless an exemption applies. California uses merit review — regulator can deny qualification based on fairness.
california.rule_506_preemption: Rule 506 offerings are preempted from California qualification but still require state notice filing and fee under Cal. Corp. Code § 25102.1

martin_act_provisions.scope: Applies to any fraud or deception in connection with the offer, sale, or purchase of securities within or from New York
covered_securities: Real estate syndications structured as securities
Digital assets and cryptocurrency (per recent enforcement)
martin_act_provisions.no_scienter_required: Unlike federal 10b-5, Martin Act does NOT require proof of intent to deceive or scienter. Materially misleading statements or omissions sufficient for civil liability.
martin_act_provisions.no_reliance_required: No requirement to prove investor reliance on the misrepresentation
martin_act_provisions.no_privity_required: AG can bring action without individual investor complaint

enforcement_powers.criminal: Felony charges available — up to 4 years imprisonment per count
enforcement_powers.investigative: Broad subpoena power to compel documents and testimony without filing charges

martin_act_provisions.no_private_right_of_action: Martin Act does NOT create a private right of action for investors (Assured Guaranty v. J.P. Morgan, 2012). Only AG can enforce.
martin_act_provisions.vc_relevance: Any VC transaction involving New York-based parties, New York law governing agreements, or solicitation of New York investors potentially subject to Martin Act jurisdiction

new_york.registration: New York uses notice filing for federal covered securities and has additional state-level exemptions for private placements under the Martin Act regulations

state_notice_filing_for_rule_506.requirement: Most states require Form D notice filings for Rule 506 offerings within 15 days of first sale to residents of that state
state_notice_filing_for_rule_506.filing_system: NASAA Electronic Filing Depository (EFD) at nasaaefd.org — most states accept electronic filing through this centralized system

typical_fees_by_state.variable: Some states charge percentage of offering amount (Arkansas: 0.1% min $100 max $500; Delaware: 0.05% min $200 max $1,000)

state_notice_filing_for_rule_506.late_filing_penalties: $50 to $2,500 depending on state and duration of delinquency
state_notice_filing_for_rule_506.practical_note: Many issuers use blue sky filing services (CT Corporation, CSC Global, National Corporate Research) to manage multi-state filings

delaware_corporate_law.purpose: Delaware General Corporation Law essentials for VC legal analysis. Covers key DGCL sections, fiduciary duties, governance document hierarchy, preferred stock mechanics, and common traps.

section_102_charter_contents.summary: Defines required and optional contents of the certificate of incorporation.
required_contents: Registered office address and registered agent in Delaware
Nature of business or purposes (may be 'any lawful act or activity')
Total number of authorized shares, par value (or statement of no par), and classes/series if applicable
Name and mailing address of each incorporator
optional_provisions_102b: 102(b)(1): Any provision for management of business, powers of the corporation/directors/stockholders, or creating/limiting/regulating director powers, provided not contrary to Delaware law
102(b)(4): Provisions restricting corporate business to stated purposes
102(b)(6): Provisions imposing personal liability on stockholders for corporate debts (rare in VC context)

102(b)(7): Exculpation clause eliminating director personal liability for monetary damages for breach of fiduciary duty of care (does NOT cover duty of loyalty, bad faith, or intentional misconduct). Extended to officers by 2022 amendment for direct claims only.
section_102_charter_contents.vc_relevance: The charter is the supreme governing document. Preferred stock designations (liquidation preference, conversion, anti-dilution, protective provisions) must be set forth in the charter or a board resolution authorized by the charter under Section 151. 102(b)(7) exculpation is standard in every VC-backed company charter.

section_109_bylaws.summary: Governs adoption, amendment, and repeal of bylaws.
key_rules: Before payment for stock: incorporators or initial directors may adopt original bylaws.
After payment for stock: stockholders entitled to vote have inherent power to adopt/amend/repeal bylaws.
Board may adopt/amend/repeal bylaws ONLY if the charter grants this power.
Bylaws may contain any provision 'relating to the business of the corporation, the conduct of its affairs, and its rights or powers' — but must not be inconsistent with law or the charter.
Stockholder-adopted bylaws can restrict the board's power to amend those specific bylaw provisions.
section_109_bylaws.charter_supremacy: Bylaws cannot override charter provisions. If a bylaw conflicts with the charter, the charter controls. This hierarchy matters when structuring protective provisions and board composition rules.
section_109_bylaws.vc_relevance: Board composition, meeting procedures, officer appointment, and indemnification advancement provisions typically live in bylaws. Post-Moelis, practitioners must be careful about what governance provisions are placed in bylaws vs charter vs stockholder agreements.

section_122_18_stockholder_agreements.summary: Enacted in direct response to the Moelis decision. Codifies board authority to enter into stockholder agreements containing governance provisions.

background.moelis_decision: West Palm Beach Firefighters' Pension Fund v. Moelis & Co. (Del. Ch., Feb. 2024) struck down stockholder agreement provisions that granted a founder-stockholder veto rights over board decisions and power to select a board majority, holding they violated Section 141(a) by improperly delegating board management authority.
background.legislative_response: Delaware legislature fast-tracked amendments effective Aug 1, 2024 to restore practitioner certainty.

permissible_provisions: Requirements for approval or consent from specified persons before the board takes certain actions
Restrictions or conditions on corporate actions
Requirements regarding board composition or nomination
Provisions regarding meetings, quorum, or voting
section_122_18_stockholder_agreements.critical_limitation: Legislative sponsors stated 'fiduciary duties trump contracts, always.' Specific performance may be unavailable if a board breaches a 122(18) agreement based on fiduciary duty. This creates tension: contract rights may be illusory without liquidated damages clauses.
section_122_18_stockholder_agreements.vc_relevance: NVCA Voting Agreement and IRA provisions rely on 122(18) for enforceability. Practitioners should consider whether protective provisions are better placed in the charter (unambiguous enforcement) or stockholder agreements (contractual flexibility but fiduciary override risk). Post-Moelis best practice: put hard consent rights in the charter, put procedural governance in stockholder agreements under 122(18).

section_141_board_powers.summary: Central provision establishing board primacy in corporate governance.

key_subsections.141_a: Business and affairs managed 'by or under the direction of' the board. Exceptions only as provided 'in this chapter or in its certificate of incorporation.' This is the foundational principle Moelis relied upon.
key_subsections.141_b: Board composition: number fixed by or per bylaws (default: all incorporators until first stockholder meeting). Quorum is majority of total unless charter/bylaws set higher (minimum one-third).
key_subsections.141_c_committees: Board may delegate authority to committees of one or more directors. Committees CANNOT: declare dividends/distributions (except per formula), approve/recommend mergers, recommend charter amendments, approve bylaw changes, or fill board vacancies. Committee actions have full board force.
key_subsections.141_d_written_consent: Board may act without a meeting if all members consent in writing (including electronic transmission).
key_subsections.141_f_reliance: Directors entitled to rely in good faith on records, reports, committees, and experts. This is the statutory basis for the business judgment rule's procedural component.
key_subsections.141_k_removal: Directors may be removed with or without cause by majority stockholder vote (unless classified board, then only for cause unless charter provides otherwise).

section_141_board_powers.vc_relevance: 141(a) is the touchstone for analyzing whether governance provisions in stockholder agreements, voting agreements, or IRA side letters improperly constrain the board. VC board observer rights, consent requirements, and committee delegation must be structured within 141 constraints.

section_144_interested_transactions.summary: Governs transactions involving conflicts of interest by directors, officers, or controlling stockholders.

march_2025_amendments.retroactivity: Applies to all acts and transactions regardless of when they occurred, but does not affect pending litigation as of February 17, 2025.

safe_harbor_requirements.interested_director_officer: Either (a) full disclosure + good faith approval by affirmative majority vote of disinterested directors/committee, OR (b) disclosure + approval by disinterested stockholders.
safe_harbor_requirements.controlling_stockholder: Higher bar — requires showing of entire fairness unless safe harbor met through MFW framework (independent committee + majority-of-minority approval, both ab initio).

section_144_interested_transactions.failure_consequence: If no safe harbor: burden shifts to conflicted party to prove 'entire fairness' — Delaware's most demanding standard.
section_144_interested_transactions.vc_relevance: Critical for VC-backed companies where investor directors sit on boards while their funds have economic interests in the company. Bridge loans from existing investors, acqui-hires of portfolio companies, downstream rounds with affiliated funds, and related-party transactions all implicate Section 144. Every conflicted transaction should be processed through a disinterested director/committee approval process with full disclosure.

sections.151_classes_and_series: Authorizes creation of classes and series of stock with distinct voting powers, designations, preferences, and special rights. Blank check preferred: charter can authorize the board to set terms of future series by resolution without stockholder vote. Redemption permitted at corporation's or holder's option, but at least one class with full voting and no redemption must remain outstanding.
sections.152_issuance_consideration: Board determines form of payment: cash, tangible/intangible property, or any benefit to the corporation (including services). Board's valuation judgment is conclusive absent actual fraud. Stock issued for adequate consideration is fully paid and nonassessable.
sections.153_par_value_consideration: Shares with par value cannot be issued for consideration less than par. Surplus above par goes to capital surplus. No-par stock: board determines what portion of consideration is capital.
sections.154_surplus_definition: Excess of net assets over stated capital. Relevant to dividend and redemption legality.
sections.155_fractional_shares: Corporation may issue fractional shares, pay cash in lieu, or issue scrip. Fractional shares carry proportional voting and dividend rights.
sections.156_partly_paid_shares: Corporation may issue partly paid shares and assess remaining balance. Rarely used in VC context.
sections.157_stock_options_warrants: Board may create and issue rights/options to acquire stock. May delegate issuance authority subject to: maximum shares, time period, and minimum consideration. Foundation for equity incentive plans and warrant issuances.

section_151_157_stock_issuance.delegation_framework: Sections 152 and 157 (amended 2015) allow delegation of stock and option issuance authority from the board to officers or other persons, provided the resolution specifies maximum shares, time period, and minimum consideration. Enables at-the-market offerings and routine equity grants without board approval of each issuance.
section_151_157_stock_issuance.vc_relevance: Section 151 is the statutory basis for all preferred stock terms — liquidation preferences, conversion rights, anti-dilution, dividends, protective provisions must all be authorized by the charter or a board resolution under 151. Section 157 governs the equity incentive pool. Failure to have sufficient authorized shares before issuance is a common defect requiring Section 204 ratification.

section_220_books_and_records.summary: Grants stockholders the right to inspect corporate books and records for a proper purpose.
section_220_books_and_records.who_can_demand: Any stockholder of record, or beneficial owner of shares held by a nominee or in a voting trust. No minimum share count or holding period required.
procedural_requirements: Written demand under oath (affirmation under penalty of perjury sufficient)
Directed to corporation's registered office in Delaware or principal place of business
Power of attorney required if made through an agent
Beneficial owners must provide documentary evidence of ownership
section_220_books_and_records.proper_purpose: Must be 'reasonably related to such person's interest as a stockholder.' Recognized purposes: valuing shares, investigating suspected mismanagement or wrongdoing, communicating with other stockholders. Denied for 'idle curiosity' or 'fishing expeditions.'
section_220_books_and_records.scope: Stock ledger, stockholder lists, and other books and records. Extends to subsidiary records the corporation possesses or can obtain. Court determines scope based on necessity.
section_220_books_and_records.credible_basis_standard: Stockholder must present a 'credible basis' to infer wrongdoing — a threshold 'somewhere below a reasonable inference' but requiring more than suspicion.
section_220_books_and_records.march_2025_amendment: Amended to address scope concerns. Changes do not apply to demands made before February 17, 2025.
section_220_books_and_records.vc_relevance: Section 220 is preserved in NVCA IRA documents. VC investors with small stakes can still demand records. Companies should maintain clean records anticipating 220 demands, which often precede derivative litigation. The credible basis standard is relatively low — a few concerning board minutes or financial irregularities may suffice.

section_228_written_consent.summary: Permits stockholder action without a meeting via written consent.
section_228_written_consent.default_rule: Unless the charter provides otherwise, any action requiring stockholder vote may be taken by written consent signed by holders of the minimum votes that would be necessary at a meeting with all shares present.
section_228_written_consent.opt_out: The charter MAY prohibit action by written consent entirely (common in public companies, rare in VC-backed private companies).
section_228_written_consent.timing: Consent must be delivered within 60 days of the earliest dated consent. Prompt notice of the action must be given to non-consenting stockholders.
section_228_written_consent.electronic_consent: Written consent includes consent transmitted by electronic means (email, DocuSign, etc.).
section_228_written_consent.vc_relevance: Written consent is the primary mechanism for stockholder approvals in VC-backed companies (charter amendments, new stock issuances, mergers). Eliminates need for formal meetings. Practitioners must track the 60-day delivery window and ensure proper notice to all non-consenting holders. The charter should preserve written consent rights — inadvertent opt-out language is a costly mistake.

section_242_charter_amendments.summary: Governs amendments to the certificate of incorporation after receipt of payment for stock.
procedure: Board adopts resolution declaring amendment advisable and calling for stockholder vote (or written consent under Section 228).
Majority of outstanding shares entitled to vote must approve (unless charter requires supermajority).
If amendment would adversely affect a class or series, that class/series gets a separate class vote even if not otherwise entitled to vote.
Certificate of amendment filed with Delaware Secretary of State.
section_242_charter_amendments.class_vote_trigger: Any amendment that 'would increase or decrease the aggregate number of authorized shares of such class, increase or decrease the par value of the shares of such class, or alter or change the powers, preferences, or special rights of the shares of such class so as to affect them adversely' requires approval of a majority of the outstanding shares of that class, voting as a separate class.
section_242_charter_amendments.2023_amendment: Reduced minimum stockholder vote for forward and reverse stock splits from majority to a lower threshold, subject to certain conditions.
section_242_charter_amendments.vc_relevance: Every financing round requiring new authorized shares triggers Section 242. Preferred stockholders have statutory class vote rights on adverse amendments independent of any contractual protective provisions. Failure to obtain required class votes renders the amendment invalid. Common trap: assuming protective provision consent replaces the statutory class vote — both are required.

section_251_mergers.summary: Establishes procedures and requirements for mergers and consolidations.
procedure: Board of each constituent corporation adopts a resolution approving an agreement of merger.
Agreement must state: terms and conditions, manner of converting shares, any charter amendments for the surviving entity.
Stockholders of each corporation must approve by majority vote of outstanding shares entitled to vote.
Certificate of merger filed with Delaware Secretary of State.
section_251_mergers.small_merger_exception_251f: Parent corporation owning 90%+ of outstanding shares of each class of a subsidiary may merge the subsidiary without subsidiary stockholder vote (short-form merger).
section_251_mergers.251_h_medium_form_merger: Permits a merger following a tender offer without a stockholder vote if: (a) the acquiring entity holds enough shares post-tender to approve the merger, (b) the merger is consummated promptly, and (c) the merger consideration equals the tender offer consideration.
section_251_mergers.vc_relevance: M&A exits for VC-backed companies proceed under Section 251. Drag-along provisions in voting agreements are designed to ensure the requisite vote is achieved. Short-form mergers (251(f)) are relevant for squeeze-out transactions. Appraisal rights under Section 262 attach to most mergers — dissenting stockholders can seek judicial fair value determination.

section_262_appraisal_rights.summary: Provides stockholders the right to seek judicial determination of 'fair value' of their shares in certain transactions.
section_262_appraisal_rights.triggering_transactions: Mergers (Section 251), consolidations, conversions, transfers, domestications, and continuances.
eligibility: Must hold shares continuously through the effective date of the transaction.
Must NOT have voted in favor of or consented to the transaction.
Must make a written demand for appraisal before the vote.
Must not withdraw demand or otherwise lose standing.
section_262_appraisal_rights.market_out_exception: Appraisal rights are NOT available for shares: (a) listed on a national securities exchange, OR (b) held of record by more than 2,000 holders — UNLESS the merger consideration is anything other than (i) shares of the surviving corporation, (ii) shares listed on a national exchange, (iii) cash in lieu of fractional shares, or (iv) any combination thereof.
section_262_appraisal_rights.fair_value: Court of Chancery determines 'fair value' — the value of shares immediately before the merger, exclusive of any value arising from the merger itself. Court has discretion on valuation methodology.
section_262_appraisal_rights.interest: Court may award interest on the fair value amount from the effective date of the merger.
section_262_appraisal_rights.vc_relevance: Appraisal rights are a backstop for minority stockholders (including dissenting preferred holders) in exits below liquidation preference. Private company stockholders are not subject to the market-out exception. Drag-along provisions must be carefully drafted to address appraisal right waivers. Courts have found fair value above and below the deal price — appraisal is a two-way risk.

section_271_asset_sales.summary: Governs sale, lease, or exchange of all or substantially all corporate assets.
requirements: Majority vote of outstanding shares entitled to vote.
section_271_asset_sales.substantially_all_test: DGCL does not define 'substantially all.' Courts apply a quantitative and qualitative analysis: does the transaction leave the corporation without a significant continuing business activity? Typically triggered when 75%+ of assets by value are transferred, but context-dependent.
section_271_asset_sales.no_appraisal_rights: Unlike mergers under Section 251, asset sales under Section 271 do NOT trigger statutory appraisal rights. This is a key structural difference that influences deal structure choice.
section_271_asset_sales.vc_relevance: Asset sales are sometimes used as alternatives to mergers to avoid appraisal rights or for tax structuring. Protective provisions in VC charters typically require preferred stockholder consent for any 'deemed liquidation event' including asset sales, providing contractual protections beyond the statutory requirements.

section_275_dissolution.summary: Establishes the procedure for voluntary dissolution.
procedure: Board adopts dissolution resolution by majority vote.
Majority of outstanding shares entitled to vote must approve at a meeting (with proper notice) or by written consent under Section 228.
Certificate of dissolution filed with Delaware Secretary of State, containing: corporate name, authorization date, director/officer information, and original incorporation date.
Alternatively: unanimous written consent of all stockholders entitled to vote (no board resolution needed).
section_275_dissolution.winding_up: After dissolution, the corporation continues to exist for three years solely for winding up: paying debts, distributing remaining assets to stockholders, and prosecuting/defending lawsuits.
section_275_dissolution.vc_relevance: Dissolution is the terminal outcome for failed portfolio companies. Liquidation preference waterfall is triggered. Protective provisions typically require preferred consent before dissolution. The three-year winding-up period means contingent liabilities persist — D&O tail coverage is essential. Preferred holders' liquidation preference is senior to common in dissolution distributions.

section_203_business_combinations.summary: Anti-takeover statute restricting business combinations with interested stockholders.
section_203_business_combinations.core_rule: Prohibits 'business combinations' with any 'interested stockholder' (holder of 15%+ of voting stock) for three years following the date the stockholder became interested.
exceptions: Board approved the transaction or the stockholder's acquisition of 15%+ BEFORE the stockholder became interested.

Upon consummation of the transaction making the stockholder interested, the stockholder owned at least 85% of voting stock (excluding shares held by officer-directors and certain employee stock plans).
After becoming interested, the business combination is approved by the board AND by 66-2/3% supermajority of disinterested outstanding voting stock at a stockholder meeting.
opt_out_mechanisms: Charter provision adopted at incorporation (or by stockholder amendment) opting out of Section 203.
Bylaw amendment adopted within 90 days of February 2, 1988 (historical).
Stockholder-approved charter or bylaw amendment (effective 12 months after adoption).
section_203_business_combinations.vc_relevance: Most VC-backed companies opt out of Section 203 in their charter because it can inadvertently block legitimate financing rounds or acquisitions where an investor crosses the 15% threshold. Failure to opt out is a common trap — a lead investor buying a large stake could trigger the three-year prohibition on follow-on transactions. Standard NVCA charter includes a Section 203 opt-out.

duty_of_care.standard: Directors must exercise the care that 'ordinarily careful and prudent' persons would use in similar circumstances.
components: Process duty: must inform themselves of all material information reasonably available before making a decision.
Oversight duty (Caremark): must implement and monitor reporting systems to remain informed about corporate operations.
duty_of_care.breach_standard: Gross negligence — a decision so uninformed or reckless as to constitute an 'abuse of discretion.' Not mere negligence or poor judgment.
duty_of_care.exculpation: Section 102(b)(7) allows charters to eliminate personal monetary liability for duty of care breaches. This is standard in all VC-backed company charters. Does NOT exculpate for duty of loyalty, bad faith, or intentional misconduct. Extended to officers (direct claims only) by 2022 amendment.

duty_of_loyalty.standard: Directors must act in good faith in the best interests of the corporation and its stockholders. Must not derive personal benefit from a position of trust at the corporation's expense.
components: Self-dealing prohibition: cannot transact with the corporation on unfair terms.
Corporate opportunity doctrine: cannot divert business opportunities properly belonging to the corporation.
Good faith: must not act with intent to violate law or consciously disregard known duties.
Candor (duty of disclosure): must disclose all material information in stockholder communications.
duty_of_loyalty.cannot_be_exculpated: Unlike duty of care, duty of loyalty breaches CANNOT be eliminated by a 102(b)(7) charter provision. This is why loyalty claims are the primary avenue for director liability in Delaware.
duty_of_loyalty.vc_context: VC directors face heightened loyalty scrutiny because they serve dual roles — as directors owing duties to all stockholders, and as fund representatives with economic incentives that may diverge from the company's interests. Conflicts arise in: downstream rounds with affiliated funds, bridge financings, recapitalizations, and sale processes.

business_judgment_rule.standard: Presumption that directors acted on an informed basis, in good faith, and in the honest belief that the action was in the corporation's best interest.
business_judgment_rule.effect: Court will not second-guess the substance of a board decision if the decision-making process was adequate. Plaintiff bears the burden of rebutting the presumption.
rebuttal_triggers: Director had a material financial interest in the transaction (self-dealing).
Director lacked independence from an interested party.
Decision was not the product of a reasonable decision-making process (gross negligence).
business_judgment_rule.corwin_cleansing: Under Corwin v. KKR Financial Holdings (Del. 2015), a fully informed, uncoerced vote of disinterested stockholders approving a transaction restores business judgment review even where enhanced scrutiny (Revlon) would otherwise apply. Powerful defense mechanism for board-approved deals.

entire_fairness.standard: Delaware's 'most onerous' standard of review. Applies when the business judgment rule is rebutted due to conflicts of interest.

two_prongs.fair_dealing: Encompasses timing, initiation, structure, negotiation, and disclosure of the transaction. Process-oriented inquiry.
two_prongs.fair_price: Whether the price falls within a range of fairness. Both prongs examined as a unitary test; neither alone is sufficient.

entire_fairness.burden: Burden of proof shifts to the defendant directors/controlling stockholder to demonstrate entire fairness. Exception: under MFW framework (Kahn v. M&F Worldwide), burden shifts back to plaintiff if the transaction was conditioned ab initio on both (a) approval by a fully empowered independent committee and (b) a majority-of-the-minority stockholder vote.
entire_fairness.vc_context: Entire fairness applies to recapitalizations, down rounds led by insiders, squeeze-out mergers, and any transaction where conflicted directors or controlling stockholders stand on both sides. VC boards should use independent committees and majority-of-minority conditions to shift the burden away from entire fairness.

revlon_duties.trigger: Enhanced scrutiny applies when the board decides to sell the company or when a change of control becomes inevitable. Named after Revlon, Inc. v. MacAndrews & Forbes Holdings (Del. 1986).
revlon_duties.standard: Board's duty shifts from long-term value preservation to obtaining the highest price reasonably available for stockholders in a sale. Board must conduct a reasonable auction or market check.
does_not_apply_when: Transaction involves a stock-for-stock merger with no change of control.
Company has a controlling stockholder (entire fairness applies instead).
Fully informed, uncoerced stockholder vote cleanses under Corwin.
revlon_duties.vc_context: Revlon duties are triggered in most M&A exits of VC-backed companies because a cash sale constitutes a change of control. Boards must demonstrate they sought the best price — a single-bidder process is defensible only if the board can show it was reasonable under the circumstances. Deal protections (no-shop, break-up fees, matching rights) are scrutinized under Revlon for reasonableness.

blasius_enhanced_scrutiny.standard: Board must demonstrate a 'compelling justification' when its primary purpose is to interfere with the stockholder franchise (voting rights, director elections).
blasius_enhanced_scrutiny.origin: Blasius Industries v. Atlas Corp. (Del. Ch. 1988) — board expanded its size and filled new seats to dilute a dissident stockholder's voting power.
blasius_enhanced_scrutiny.coster_refinement: In Coster v. UIP Companies (Del. 2023), the Delaware Supreme Court held Blasius is properly subsumed within Unocal's threat-and-response analysis but remains a vital and independent review standard. Board actions that interfere with elections are 'twice-tested' for both legal validity and equitable fairness.
blasius_enhanced_scrutiny.application: Applies to: defensive board expansions, acceleration of meeting dates to thwart proxies, adoption of supermajority voting requirements, poison pills that disenfranchise specific stockholders.
blasius_enhanced_scrutiny.vc_context: Relevant when VC-backed company founders or investors attempt to manipulate board composition to entrench control — e.g., expanding the board to fill new seats with allies before a contested election, or amending bylaws to change advance notice requirements to block a stockholder's director nominees.

governance_document_hierarchy.overview: Delaware governance operates through a strict hierarchy: DGCL statute > charter > bylaws > stockholder agreements. Each level is constrained by those above it.

certificate_of_incorporation_charter.authority: Supreme corporate governance document, second only to the DGCL itself.
what_must_live_here: Authorized share count and classes/series designations
Preferred stock rights, preferences, and privileges (Section 151)
Protective provisions granting class-specific veto rights (statutory class vote rights are automatic under Section 242, but additional contractual veto rights are placed here)
Any restriction or expansion of default DGCL rules (e.g., supermajority voting requirements)
certificate_of_incorporation_charter.amendment_requirements: Board resolution + majority vote of outstanding shares entitled to vote + class vote if any class is adversely affected. Cannot be amended by board alone.
certificate_of_incorporation_charter.post_moelis_significance: Charter provisions are unambiguously enforceable. Governance rights placed in the charter are not subject to the fiduciary-duty-trumps-contract limitation that affects stockholder agreements under 122(18).

bylaws.authority: Subordinate to charter and DGCL. Cannot be inconsistent with either.
what_typically_lives_here: Advance notice requirements for stockholder proposals and director nominations
Forum selection clauses (Delaware Court of Chancery)
bylaws.amendment_by_board: Board can amend bylaws only if charter grants this power (standard in VC charters). Stockholders always retain power to amend, and can adopt bylaw provisions restricting future board amendments.
bylaws.limitation: Bylaws govern procedure, not substance. Cannot restrict core board management authority under Section 141(a). Cannot grant stockholders veto rights over business decisions — that belongs in the charter.

stockholder_agreements_post_moelis.authority: Contractual arrangements between the corporation and its stockholders. Enforceability now governed by Section 122(18).
what_can_live_here_post_122_18: Board composition and nomination rights (investor director designation)
Consent and approval requirements for specified corporate actions
stockholder_agreements_post_moelis.critical_limitation: Fiduciary duties override contractual obligations. A board may breach a stockholder agreement provision if complying would violate fiduciary duties. Remedy for breach may be limited to damages rather than specific performance.
best_practices_post_moelis: Place hard economic protections (liquidation preference, anti-dilution, protective provision vetoes) in the charter where enforcement is unambiguous.
Place governance mechanics (board nomination process, information delivery, transfer restrictions, registration rights) in stockholder agreements under 122(18).
Include liquidated damages clauses in stockholder agreements to address the fiduciary-override gap.
Ensure stockholder agreements contain express authorization language referencing Section 122(18).
Avoid provisions that completely divest the board of discretion over core management functions — even post-122(18), extreme constraints may be challenged.

board_can_do_unilaterally: Set terms of blank-check preferred series (Section 151, if charter authorizes)
Approve interested transactions (Section 144 safe harbor with disinterested director vote)
requires_stockholder_approval: Mergers and consolidations (Section 251, except short-form under 251(f))
Increase in authorized shares (charter amendment under Section 242)
New class/series of stock with rights superior to existing preferred (triggers class vote)

preferred_stock_mechanics.statutory_basis: Section 151 authorizes the charter to set forth or authorize the board to set rights, preferences, privileges, and restrictions for each class/series. All preferred stock terms must ultimately trace back to the charter or a board resolution authorized by the charter.

conversion_rights.optional_conversion: Holder may convert preferred shares into common at any time at the applicable conversion ratio. Initially 1:1, adjusted by anti-dilution provisions.
conversion_rights.automatic_conversion: Preferred converts to common upon a Qualified IPO (typically defined by minimum offering size and price per share, e.g., $50M+ at 2-3x original issue price). Once triggered, mandatory for all holders of that series.
conversion_rights.conversion_ratio_adjustments: BBWA anti-dilution adjusts the conversion ratio upon issuance of common stock below the applicable conversion price. Full ratchet adjusts to the lowest price per share regardless of dilution magnitude (effectively extinct in US VC).
conversion_rights.charter_requirement: Conversion mechanics MUST be in the charter (certificate of incorporation). They define the fundamental economic terms of the preferred stock under Section 151.

redemption.statutory_framework: Section 151(b) permits redemption at the corporation's option, the holder's option, or upon occurrence of a specified event. At least one class of common stock with full voting rights must remain outstanding and unredeemable.
redemption.legal_availability_limitation: Delaware courts imply a 'legally available funds' requirement even when the charter contains a mandatory redemption provision. Corporation cannot redeem if doing so would render it insolvent or unable to pay debts as they come due (ThoughtWorks and SV Innovation Partners precedent).
redemption.burden_of_proof: Preferred stockholders seeking to enforce mandatory redemption bear the burden of proving the corporation has legally available funds. The corporation need not prove insolvency.
redemption.charter_vs_contract: Redemption rights in the charter bind the corporation directly. Redemption rights in a stockholder agreement create contractual obligations subject to the fiduciary override limitation.
redemption.vc_practice: Mandatory redemption is declining in frequency but still appears in some Series A+ deals (typically 5-7 year trigger). Functions as a 'put right' forcing a liquidity event. Practically difficult to enforce against a cash-strapped startup.

dividends.cumulative_vs_noncumulative: Cumulative: unpaid dividends accrue and must be paid before common receives any distribution. Non-cumulative: if board does not declare, the right lapses for that period. NVCA standard is non-cumulative.
dividends.declaration_requirement: Under Delaware law, dividends on preferred stock are payable only when, as, and if declared by the board — even for cumulative dividends. The board cannot be compelled to declare dividends absent express charter language creating a mandatory obligation.
dividends.surplus_requirement: Dividends may only be paid out of surplus (Section 170). If insufficient surplus, dividends may be paid from net profits for the current and/or preceding fiscal year.
dividends.charter_requirement: Dividend preferences and rights must be set forth in the charter under Section 151.

protective_provisions.nature: Negative covenants requiring affirmative consent of the holders of a specified percentage of preferred stock before the corporation takes enumerated actions.

charter_vs_contractual.charter_provisions: Protective provisions in the charter operate as conditions on corporate power. The corporation literally cannot take the action without the consent. Violation renders the action void or voidable. Enforcement is unambiguous.
charter_vs_contractual.contractual_provisions_ira: Protective provisions placed in the IRA or voting agreement are contractual obligations. Post-Moelis, enforcement may be limited if a board argues fiduciary duties required the action despite lack of consent. Remedy may be damages rather than injunction.
charter_vs_contractual.best_practice: Place all protective provisions requiring investor veto in the charter. Use the IRA/stockholder agreement for affirmative covenants (information delivery, governance policies) rather than veto rights.

standard_nvca_list: Alter or change rights, preferences, or privileges of preferred stock
Increase or decrease authorized shares of any class/series of preferred
Create or authorize any new class/series senior to or pari passu with existing preferred
Redeem or repurchase shares (except employee repurchases at cost)
Declare or pay dividends on any shares
Change the authorized number of board members
Liquidate, dissolve, or wind up the corporation; effect any deemed liquidation event
Amend the charter or bylaws in a manner adverse to the preferred
Increase or decrease the size of the option pool
Incur indebtedness above a specified threshold

unauthorized_stock_issuance.trap: Issuing shares in excess of authorized capital in the charter, or issuing a new series of preferred without properly filing the certificate of designation first.
unauthorized_stock_issuance.consequence: Shares issued without authorization are void or voidable. Void shares never existed and cannot be ratified without a court order (Section 205). Voidable shares can be ratified under Section 204.
section_204_ratification: Board resolution identifying the defect and approving ratification.
Stockholder approval (by the vote that would have been required at the time of the original act).
If a class had consent rights at the time of the defect, that class must consent to ratification even if it no longer holds such rights.
Certificate of Validation filed with Delaware Secretary of State.
unauthorized_stock_issuance.prevention: Always confirm authorized share counts before any issuance. File certificates of designation before closing. Track authorized vs issued vs reserved shares in a cap table reconciliation.

improper_charter_amendments.trap: Amending the charter without obtaining required class votes under Section 242, or without obtaining protective provision consents from preferred stockholders.
improper_charter_amendments.key_distinction: Section 242 class votes are STATUTORY — they exist regardless of what the charter says. Protective provision consents are CONTRACTUAL — they exist because the charter grants them. Both must be obtained. One does not substitute for the other.
common_scenarios: Increasing authorized common to expand the option pool without preferred class vote (if it adversely affects preferred).
Changing preferred terms in a recapitalization without obtaining consent from each affected series individually.
Assuming the 'majority of preferred voting as a single class' threshold covers series-specific consent requirements.
improper_charter_amendments.consequence: Amendment is void or voidable. Subsequent transactions relying on the amendment (e.g., stock issuances from increased authorized shares) are also potentially defective.

failure_to_obtain_required_consents.trap: Proceeding with a corporate action without identifying and obtaining all required approvals — board, stockholder, class-specific, and contractual.
common_consent_failures: Not obtaining protective provision consent before incurring debt, changing board size, or entering related-party transactions.
Failing to obtain Section 228 written consent from all required holders within the 60-day window.
Not providing notice to non-consenting stockholders after action by written consent.
Missing a contractual consent right buried in a side letter or investor rights agreement.
Failing to obtain spousal consent on community property shares.
failure_to_obtain_required_consents.prevention: Maintain a consent checklist for every material corporate action. Cross-reference: charter protective provisions, IRA/voting agreement covenants, board committee charter requirements, and any side letter obligations.

section_203_trap.trap: Failing to opt out of Section 203 in the charter, then having a lead investor cross the 15% voting stock threshold, which triggers a three-year prohibition on business combinations with that investor.
section_203_trap.vc_specific_risk: A Series A lead investor acquiring 20% of outstanding voting stock becomes an 'interested stockholder.' Without a prior board approval or charter opt-out, the company cannot merge with or sell substantially all assets to that investor (or any entity affiliated with that investor) for three years.
section_203_trap.additional_trap: Support agreements and rollover agreements in M&A transactions may collectively push stockholder groups above 15%, triggering Section 203 even in negotiated deals. Plaintiffs have increasingly used this theory to challenge friendly mergers.
section_203_trap.prevention: Include a Section 203 opt-out in the initial charter at incorporation. Verify opt-out is in place during every financing round diligence. If not present, amend the charter to opt out before the investor crosses 15%.

deemed_liquidation_event_gaps.trap: Charter's 'deemed liquidation event' definition fails to capture all exit scenarios, or captures unintended scenarios (e.g., TGE in crypto companies, asset spin-offs, exclusive IP licenses).
deemed_liquidation_event_gaps.consequence: If a transaction is not a deemed liquidation event, preferred stockholders' liquidation preference is not triggered, and they receive proceeds pari passu with common. If a transaction is unintentionally captured, it may require preferred supermajority consent under protective provisions.
deemed_liquidation_event_gaps.prevention: Define deemed liquidation events precisely. Standard NVCA definition covers mergers, asset sales, and exclusive IP licenses. Crypto companies must carve out TGE. Ensure alignment between charter DLE definition and protective provision consent requirements.

forum_selection_gaps.trap: Failing to include a forum selection clause designating the Delaware Court of Chancery as the exclusive forum for internal corporate claims.
forum_selection_gaps.consequence: Stockholder litigation can be filed in any jurisdiction, increasing cost, unpredictability, and the risk of inconsistent rulings.
forum_selection_gaps.prevention: Include a Delaware Court of Chancery exclusive forum clause in both the charter and bylaws. Post-Salzberg v. Sciabacucchi (Del. 2020), federal forum provisions for Securities Act claims may also be included in the charter.

cooley.key_resource: Quarterly Venture Financing Reports (richest public US deal term data)
cooley.data_depth: 258 deals in Q4 2024 alone, tracks liquidation pref, anti-dilution, pay-to-play quarterly since 2014
cooley.tools: CooleyGO document generators (NVCA suite, SAFE, convertible note, Series Seed)
cooley.ai_deals: #1 in AI VC deal volume Q4 2024, $17.7B+ across 450+ deals

gunderson_dettmer.key_resource: Annual and mid-year VC reports (most venture financings by deal count)
gunderson_dettmer.training: Catalyze platform with multi-part GC Primer covering full venture financing process
gunderson_dettmer.data_point: Pre-seed median post-money caps hit $15M (20% above 2021) in 2024

wilson_sonsini.key_resource: Quarterly Entrepreneurs Reports from proprietary database
wilson_sonsini.tools: Free term sheet generators (equity financing + convertible note) via FAST tool
wilson_sonsini.knowledge_base: ECVC hub with 200+ FAQs from formation through exit

fenwick_west.key_resource: Venture Beacon (formerly Silicon Valley VC Survey) — longest-running deal term survey
fenwick_west.data_depth: Tracks provision-level data including founder preferred stock (rising 6% to 11%), SAFE usage (91% pre-seed)
fenwick_west.ai_deals: Represented Lightspeed in Anthropic $3.5B Series E

latham_watkins.key_resource: Book of Jargon: Emerging Companies glossary (web/app)
latham_watkins.strength: Growth-stage and pre-IPO stronger than early-stage benchmarking
latham_watkins.additional: Private M&A Market Study for growth-stage exits

kirkland_ellis.key_resource: Levin/Rocap treatise: Structuring VC/PE Transactions (1,400 pages, updated annually since 1994)
kirkland_ellis.strength: Late-stage VC and PE crossover. Mega-infrastructure deals.
kirkland_ellis.ai_deals: Handles mega-infra deals ($10B Firmus, OpenAI partnership structures)

goodwin_procter.key_resource: 15-Minute Founder's Guide series, dedicated pay-to-play guide (Aug 2025)
goodwin_procter.data_point: #1 global VC advisor by deal count (LSEG 2024)

orrick.key_resource: Deal Flow 5.0 — best European VC deal terms analysis (375+ deals, 10K+ data points)
orrick.data_point: Secondaries in 30% of rounds; ASAs/SAFEs now 63% of European convertibles

cross_firm_consensus_2025: 1x non-participating liquidation preference: 96-98% of deals. Universal.
BBWA anti-dilution: 100% in recent data. Full ratchet effectively extinct in US.
Pay-to-play: elevated at 6-10% but still minority.
Board sizes compressing at growth stage (7 to 6 at Series C+).
Market is bifurcated: AI premiums vs non-AI distress creating divergent term structures.

qsbs_section_1202.summary: Allows exclusion of gain on sale of qualified small business stock. Most significant startup tax benefit for founders and early investors.
qualification_requirements: Stock must be in a domestic C corporation (S-corps, LLCs, partnerships do not qualify)
Acquired at original issuance directly from the corporation (not secondary market)
Acquired in exchange for money, property (not stock), or services
Held by non-corporate taxpayers (individuals, certain trusts, estates; corporations excluded)
At least 80% of corporate assets used in active conduct of qualified trade or business during substantially all of holding period
Corporation must meet aggregate gross assets test at time of issuance

active_business_test.requirement: At least 80% of assets (by value) used in active conduct of one or more qualified trades or businesses
excluded_businesses: Professional services (health, law, engineering, architecture, accounting, actuarial science, performing arts, consulting, athletics, financial services, brokerage)
Banking, insurance, financing, leasing, investing, or similar financial businesses
Mining and extraction (products subject to percentage depletion)
Hospitality (hotels, motels, restaurants, or similar businesses)
active_business_test.note: Technology, SaaS, biotech, hardware, and most startup verticals qualify. Service businesses are the primary exclusion risk.

post_obbba.applies_to: Stock issued on or after July 4, 2025

aggregate_gross_assets_test.calculation: Cash plus aggregate adjusted basis of other property held by corporation at any time before and immediately after issuance
aggregate_gross_assets_test.subsidiary_rule: Assets of subsidiaries (>50% owned by voting or value) are consolidated into calculation
aggregate_gross_assets_test.contributed_property: Property contributed with carryover basis is treated at fair market value for this test

pre_obbba.cap: Greater of $10 million or 10x adjusted basis, per issuer per taxpayer (lifetime)

post_obbba.applies_to: Stock issued on or after July 4, 2025
post_obbba.cap: Greater of $15 million or 10x adjusted basis, per issuer per taxpayer (lifetime)

stacking_across_entities.mechanism: The exclusion cap is per-taxpayer, per-issuer. Transferring QSBS to other taxpayers (via gifts to non-grantor trusts, family members, or heirs) allows each recipient to claim their own exclusion.
stacking_across_entities.section_1202h: Gift recipients and heirs step into the shoes of the original holder — QSBS status and holding period carry over
planning_strategies: Gifts to non-grantor trusts (each trust gets its own exclusion)
Gifts to family members (each gets own exclusion, but 1202(h) tacking applies)
Transfers at death (heirs inherit QSBS status with tacked holding period)
Multiple trusts for different beneficiaries to multiply exclusion
limitations: Grantor trusts do not create a separate taxpayer — no stacking benefit
Transfers to corporations disqualify the stock
Must be bona fide transfers, not step transactions

obbba_2025_changes.law: One Big Beautiful Bill Act (H.R. 1, 119th Congress)
obbba_2025_changes.effective_date: Stock issued on or after July 4, 2025 (pre-existing stock grandfathered under prior rules)
key_changes: Introduced tiered holding periods (3/4/5 years) replacing flat 5-year requirement
Increased gross assets threshold from $50M to $75M (inflation-indexed from 2026)
Increased exclusion cap from $10M to $15M (inflation-indexed from 2027)
Enables earlier partial exclusions for exits before 5-year mark
deal_implications: Earlier exits (3-4 year holds) now receive partial QSBS benefit — changes secondary sale and M&A timing calculus
Higher gross asset threshold allows later-stage companies to issue qualifying stock
Higher exclusion cap benefits founders and early investors with large gains
Grandfathering creates two parallel regimes — must track issuance date per share lot

deal_structuring_flags: Verify C-corp status (conversion from LLC/S-corp triggers reissuance analysis)
Monitor aggregate gross assets during fundraising — a large round can breach the threshold
Active business test is ongoing — pivots into excluded businesses can retroactively disqualify
Secondary sales may break original issuance requirement unless structured properly
SAFE/convertible note conversion generally counts as original issuance at conversion date
Stock received in tax-free reorganizations can inherit QSBS status under Section 1202(h)

section_83b_elections.summary: Allows recipients of restricted property to elect immediate taxation at grant-date FMV rather than at vesting. Critical for startup founders receiving restricted stock.

core_mechanics.without_83b: Taxed as ordinary income at each vesting date based on then-current FMV. No additional capital gains on vested shares.
core_mechanics.with_83b: Pay ordinary income tax immediately on grant-date FMV (often near zero for early-stage founders). All subsequent appreciation taxed as capital gains. Starts long-term capital gains clock and QSBS holding period.

deadline.type: Calendar days from date property is transferred (not grant date if different)
deadline.extensions: None. No exceptions. No late filing relief. Missing this deadline is permanent and irreversible.
deadline.weekend_holiday: If 30th day falls on weekend or holiday, deadline is the next business day (standard IRC rules)

filing_requirements.primary: File with the IRS Service Center for taxpayer's jurisdiction
methods: Certified mail with return receipt requested (gold standard for proof of timely filing)
Electronic filing via Form 15620 (available since April 2025)
copies: Copy attached to taxpayer's next income tax return

applicable_to: Restricted stock awards (RSAs) subject to vesting
Early-exercised stock options (ISOs or NSOs exercised before vesting)
Stock purchased subject to repurchase rights
not_applicable_to: Restricted stock units (RSUs) — no property transferred at grant
Stock options that have not been exercised

founder_vesting_implications.typical_scenario: Founder receives restricted stock at incorporation when FMV is nominal ($0.0001/share). Files 83(b) within 30 days. Pays negligible tax. All future appreciation is capital gains.
founder_vesting_implications.tax_savings_example: Without 83(b): 1M shares vesting over 4 years, company valued at $10M at final vesting = ~$2.5M ordinary income per year. With 83(b): ~$100 ordinary income at grant, entire gain at exit taxed at 20% LTCG rate.
founder_vesting_implications.qsbs_interaction: Filing 83(b) starts the 5-year QSBS holding clock at grant date rather than at each vesting date. Critical for achieving full exclusion.
key_decisions: Almost always file for founders receiving stock at incorporation (FMV near zero)
Analyze more carefully for later-stage grants where FMV is already substantial
Consider forfeiture risk — if founder departs before vesting, taxes paid on forfeited shares are not refundable (limited capital loss deduction)

common_mistakes: Missing the 30-day deadline (most common and most costly — no remedy exists)
Filing with the wrong IRS office
Failing to retain proof of filing (creates audit risk years later)
Not providing a copy to the issuing company
Confusing RSUs with restricted stock (83(b) does not apply to RSUs)
Electing on stock with a high current FMV without considering forfeiture risk
Failing to report the election on the annual tax return
Not filing when exercising unvested stock options early

section_409a.summary: Governs deferred compensation arrangements including stock options. Requires options be granted at or above fair market value. Non-compliance triggers severe penalties on the option holder.
section_409a.core_rule: Nonqualified deferred compensation must comply with Section 409A rules regarding timing of deferral elections, distribution events, and acceleration prohibitions. Stock options granted below FMV are treated as deferred compensation subject to 409A.

stock_option_pricing.requirement: Exercise price must be set at or above the FMV of the underlying stock on the grant date
stock_option_pricing.consequence_of_below_fmv: Options are treated as nonqualified deferred compensation. Each vesting event triggers immediate income inclusion plus penalties.
stock_option_pricing.board_discretion: Board cannot simply pick a price — must use a reasonable valuation method or obtain an independent appraisal

409a_valuation.purpose: Establish defensible fair market value for common stock at the time of option grants

safe_harbor_methods.name: Independent Appraisal
safe_harbor_methods.description: Valuation by a qualified independent appraiser (most common for funded startups). Creates rebuttable presumption of reasonableness.
safe_harbor_methods.validity: 12 months from valuation date, absent material events

safe_harbor_methods.name: Written Report by Qualified Individual (Illiquid Startup)
safe_harbor_methods.description: For startups with no material trade or business for <10 years, no class of securities traded on public market, and no put/call on stock. Report by person with significant knowledge and experience.
safe_harbor_methods.requirements: Company must have been in existence <10 years with no public securities

safe_harbor_methods.name: Formula-Based Valuation (Binding Formula)
safe_harbor_methods.description: Consistent use of a formula (book value, multiple of earnings, etc.) for all transfers. Rarely used in VC-backed companies.

valuation_approaches: Market approach (comparable public companies, precedent transactions)
Income approach (DCF, present value of future cash flows)
Asset approach (net asset value, adjusted book value)
common_adjustments: Discount for lack of marketability (DLOM) — typically 20-35% for private companies
Option pricing model (OPM) backsolve from most recent funding round
Probability-weighted expected return method (PWERM) for multiple scenarios

material_events_requiring_new_valuation: New priced equity financing round (Series A, B, C, etc.)
Significant SAFE/convertible note issuance with large valuation cap
Material revenue milestones (major customer wins/losses, rapid growth/decline)
M&A activity, LOIs, or strategic transactions
Secondary stock transactions at known prices
Significant changes to business model or market conditions

on_service_provider: Immediate income inclusion upon vesting (not exercise)
20% additional federal tax on amounts includible in income
Interest at the underpayment rate plus 1 percentage point, from the date of vesting
Potential state penalties (California imposes an additional 20% penalty)
on_company: Reputational risk and employee morale impact
IRS correction programs available but complex and time-limited

deal_structuring_flags: Verify 409A valuation is current before any option grant
Trigger new valuation immediately after a priced round closes
Option repricing or exchange programs require 409A analysis
Acceleration of vesting in M&A context has 409A implications
Change-in-control provisions must align with 409A distribution rules
Deferred compensation in employment agreements (severance, bonuses) subject to 409A
Founders issuing options without a 409A valuation creates retroactive compliance risk for the entire option pool

carried_interest_and_fund_structure.topic: Carried Interest and VC Fund Structure
carried_interest_and_fund_structure.summary: Tax treatment of fund manager compensation in the 2-and-20 model. Section 1061 imposes a 3-year holding period for long-term capital gains treatment on carried interest.

management_fee.rate: Typically 2% of committed capital (sometimes 2.5% for smaller funds)
management_fee.tax_treatment: Ordinary income to the GP/management company
common_variations: Management fee waiver into fund interests (converts ordinary income to capital gains, subject to 1061)

carried_interest.rate: Typically 20% of fund profits above preferred return
carried_interest.preferred_return: Usually 8% annualized (the hurdle rate LPs must receive first)
carried_interest.catch_up: GP typically receives 100% of profits after preferred return until caught up to 20% of all profits
carried_interest.clawback: GP obligation to return excess carry if later investments underperform
carried_interest.tax_treatment: Capital gains if Section 1061 holding period met; otherwise ordinary income

section_1061.rule: Gains allocated to an Applicable Partnership Interest (API) must meet a 3-year holding period (not the standard 1-year) for long-term capital gains treatment
section_1061.applicable_to: Partnership interests held by taxpayers performing substantial services in applicable trades or businesses (raising/returning capital, investing in securities, real estate, commodities)
section_1061.holding_period_reference: Measured by the fund's holding period of the underlying assets, not the GP's holding period of the partnership interest
section_1061.recharacterization: Gains on assets held ≤3 years are recharacterized as short-term capital gains (taxed at ordinary income rates up to 37%, vs. 20% LTCG rate)
section_1061.capital_interest_exception: Returns attributable to GP's contributed capital (not services) are excluded from 1061 if allocations are consistent with those to non-service LPs
excluded_from_recharacterization: Capital contributed by the GP (capital interest exception)
section_1061.gp_commitment: Typical GP commits 1-5% of fund capital. Returns on this committed capital are not subject to 1061 recharacterization.

gp_lp_structure_implications.standard_structure: LP (limited partners / investors) contribute capital. GP (general partner / fund manager) manages and receives carry. Management company employs team and receives management fees.
gp_lp_structure_implications.tax_flow_through: Partnership income/loss flows through to partners on K-1. Character of income (capital gains vs ordinary) preserved at partner level.
deal_term_implications: 3-year hold requirement influences VC exit timing (pressure to hold portfolio companies >3 years)
Early exits (<3 years) result in carry taxed at ordinary income rates — affects GP economics
Management fee waiver programs subject to 1061 scrutiny
Waterfall structure (deal-by-deal vs whole-fund) affects timing of carry distributions and 1061 analysis
GP clawback obligations may need to account for tax differential if carry was distributed as LTCG but underlying gains later recharacterized

proposed_legislation_2025.bill: Carried Interest Fairness Act of 2025 (introduced February 2025)
proposed_changes: Tax all carried interest income at ordinary income rates regardless of holding period
Extend to non-U.S. corporations, SPACs, and S corporations
Subject carried interest to self-employment taxes
Significantly more aggressive than current Section 1061
proposed_legislation_2025.note: This bill has been introduced in various forms in multiple prior sessions without passing. Monitor but do not assume enactment.

state_tax_considerations.topic: State Tax Considerations for VC-Backed Companies
state_tax_considerations.summary: State tax obligations vary significantly by jurisdiction. California, Delaware, and emerging states each present distinct considerations for incorporation, operations, and investor tax treatment.

franchise_tax.minimum: $800 per year for all entities doing business in California or registered with Secretary of State
franchise_tax.llc_fee: Additional gross receipts fee of $900-$11,790 for LLCs with California-sourced income exceeding $250,000
nexus_thresholds: California sales exceed $690,000 (or 25% of worldwide sales)
California property exceeds $69,000 (or 25% of worldwide property)
California payroll exceeds $69,000 (or 25% of worldwide payroll)

income_tax.corporate_rate: 8.84% on net income (no deduction for federal taxes paid)
income_tax.individual_top_rate: 13.3% on income over ~$1M (highest state rate in the US)
income_tax.capital_gains: Taxed as ordinary income — California does not have a preferential capital gains rate
income_tax.qsbs_treatment: California does NOT conform to federal Section 1202 exclusion. QSBS gains are fully taxable at state level.

vc_reporting_requirements.law: Fair Investment Practices by Venture Capital Companies Law (FIPVCC)
requirements: Registration with California DFPI ($175 filing fee)
Applies to VC funds, SPVs, family offices with California nexus
nexus_triggers: Soliciting or receiving investments from California residents
Making VC investments in California-based businesses
Significant presence or operational office in California

incorporation_advantages: Highly developed corporate law (Court of Chancery)
Familiarity to VC investors and counsel
No state income tax on out-of-state operations

methods: Authorized shares method (can result in very high taxes for companies with large authorized share counts)
Assumed par value capital method (usually lower for startups)
franchise_tax.minimum: $400 per year plus $50 annual report fee
franchise_tax.common_pitfall: Startups with large authorized share counts (common for option pools) can owe $50,000+ under the default authorized shares method. Must elect assumed par value capital method.

delaware.no_qsbs_impact: Delaware does not have a state income tax for corporations operating out-of-state. No separate QSBS analysis needed at Delaware state level.

remote_company_nexus_issues.general_rule: Remote employees create nexus in the states where they work, potentially subjecting the company to income tax, franchise tax, payroll tax, and sales tax obligations in each state
key_considerations: Each remote employee may trigger nexus in their home state
Post-Wayfair economic nexus rules apply to sales tax even without physical presence
Apportionment formulas vary by state (single-factor sales, three-factor, etc.)
Some states have temporary remote worker nexus relief provisions (check current status)
impact_on_deals: Acquirer due diligence will assess uncollected state tax exposure
Multi-state operations complicate QSBS analysis (active business test by state)
State tax liabilities can be material in M&A purchase price adjustments

other_favorable_states.wyoming: No state income tax, no franchise tax, low filing fees, strong asset protection. Increasingly used for holding companies and crypto entities.
other_favorable_states.nevada: No state income tax, no franchise tax on most entities, strong privacy protections. Commerce tax applies if revenue exceeds $4M.
other_favorable_states.texas: No state income tax. Franchise (margin) tax applies if revenue exceeds $2.47M. Growing tech hub with favorable regulatory environment.

international_tax.topic: International Tax Considerations for VC Deals
international_tax.summary: Foreign investor participation, offshore structures, and cross-border transactions create complex tax and regulatory obligations.

cfius.full_name: Committee on Foreign Investment in the United States
cfius.scope: Reviews foreign investments in US businesses for national security risks. Voluntary filings but can mandate review or unwinding of transactions.
covered_transactions: Acquisitions giving foreign person control of a US business
