Non-controlling investments in US businesses involved in critical technology, critical infrastructure, or sensitive personal data (TID US businesses)
Real estate transactions near sensitive military/government sites

known_investor_program_2026.status: Under development — Treasury issued Request for Information February 6, 2026 (comments due March 18, 2026)
eligibility: Notified CFIUS of at least 3 covered transactions in past 3 years
Expect to submit at least 1 covered transaction in next 12 months
Not organized in or headquartered in adversary countries (China, Cuba, Iran, North Korea, Russia, Venezuelan regime)
known_investor_program_2026.benefit: Expedited review process for qualifying investors

vc_deal_implications: Foreign LP investors in VC funds can trigger CFIUS review if fund makes covered investments
Broad investor rights (board seats, protective provisions, information rights) can constitute 'control' or 'covered investment'
Limit overbroad rights for foreign investors in sensitive sectors
Early screening of cap table for foreign ownership is recommended
CFIUS risk increases for AI, defense tech, biotech, semiconductors, critical infrastructure companies

firpta.full_name: Foreign Investment in Real Property Tax Act
firpta.scope: Imposes US tax on foreign persons' gains from disposition of US real property interests (USRPIs)
firpta.usrphc_test: A US corporation is a US Real Property Holding Corporation if US real property interests comprise 50%+ of the FMV of its total assets (real property + trade/business assets + worldwide assets)

vc_relevance.general_rule: Most VC-backed tech companies are NOT USRPHCs because their value is in IP, not real property
vc_relevance.exception: Companies with significant real estate holdings, data center ownership, or other real property may trigger FIRPTA
vc_relevance.cleansing_transactions: A USRPHC can dispose of real property interests to fall below the 50% threshold before a sale event

planning_considerations: Foreign investors should assess FIRPTA exposure before investing in companies with significant real property
Blocker corporations (C-corps) can be used to shield foreign LPs from FIRPTA withholding in fund structures
FIRPTA does not apply to interests in domestically controlled REITs (ownership >50% US persons)
Treaty benefits may reduce or eliminate FIRPTA withholding for investors from treaty countries

treaty_considerations.relevance: US tax treaties may reduce withholding taxes on dividends, interest, and royalties paid to foreign investors
common_treaty_benefits: Reduced dividend withholding (often 15% reduced to 5% for substantial shareholders)
Reduced interest withholding (often 0% under many treaties)
Capital gains exemptions for non-real-property sales in many treaties
Limitation on Benefits (LOB) provisions to prevent treaty shopping
treaty_considerations.vc_application: Foreign investors in VC funds may benefit from treaty-reduced withholding on portfolio company dividends and certain exit proceeds

subpart_f_and_offshore_structures.cfc_definition: A foreign corporation is a CFC if US shareholders (each owning ≥10%) collectively own >50% of voting power or value

types: Foreign personal holding company income (interest, dividends, royalties, rents, gains from property sales)
subpart_f_income.taxation: Included in US shareholders' gross income currently (no deferral), taxed at ordinary rates up to 37%
subpart_f_income.de_minimis_exception: Subpart F income excluded if less than the lesser of 5% of CFC gross income or $1 million

gilti.rule: US shareholders of CFCs must include GILTI in income annually. Calculated as CFC tested income minus 10% of qualified business asset investment (QBAI).
gilti.deduction: 50% deduction available to C-corp shareholders under Section 250 (effective 10.5% rate). Individuals taxed at full ordinary rates unless Section 962 election made.

planning_tools: Section 962 election (allows individuals to be taxed at corporate rates on CFC inclusions)
GILTI High-Tax Exclusion (excludes income subject to foreign tax rate >18.9%)
Check-the-box elections (reclassify foreign entities to optimize structure)
Blocker corporations (interpose US C-corp to manage CFC/PFIC exposure for foreign LPs)
subpart_f_and_offshore_structures.vc_fund_structure_note: Offshore fund vehicles (Cayman feeder funds) must be structured carefully to avoid CFC or PFIC classification for US investors. Parallel fund structures (domestic + offshore) are common to manage these issues.

token_crypto_tax.summary: IRS treats crypto tokens as property. Key events in the VC/crypto lifecycle each have distinct tax treatment.

irs_classification.rule: Virtual currency is treated as property for federal income tax purposes (IRS Notice 2014-21, Rev. Rul. 2019-24, Rev. Rul. 2023-14)
irs_classification.implication: General tax principles applicable to property transactions apply. Each disposition is a taxable event.

token_sale_or_swap.treatment: Capital gain or loss measured against cost basis
token_sale_or_swap.holding_period: Short-term (≤1 year, up to 37% rate) vs. long-term (>1 year, 0/15/20% rate)
token_sale_or_swap.basis_tracking: As of January 1, 2025, IRS requires wallet-by-wallet basis allocation. Specific identification allowed if properly documented before transfer.

staking_rewards.treatment: Ordinary income at FMV when taxpayer obtains dominion and control (Rev. Rul. 2023-14)
staking_rewards.timing: Taxed when received, not when sold
staking_rewards.basis: FMV at receipt becomes cost basis for future capital gains calculation
staking_rewards.note: Applies to both direct staking and staking through exchanges/platforms

airdrops.treatment: Ordinary income at FMV when taxpayer obtains dominion and control
types_covered: Hard fork airdrops (if new coins received and accessible)
airdrops.basis: FMV at receipt becomes cost basis
airdrops.reporting: Must self-report even without broker tax forms

token_warrant_exercise.treatment: Analogous to property acquisition. Exercise price is part of cost basis. If warrant was received for services, spread at exercise may be ordinary income.
token_warrant_exercise.timing: Taxable event occurs when tokens are received and taxpayer has dominion and control
token_warrant_exercise.vc_context: Token warrants issued alongside SAFE/equity rounds — exercise at TGE creates a taxable event

tge_token_generation_event.treatment: If tokens are newly minted and distributed to holders of SAFTs or token warrants, the receipt is a taxable event
tge_token_generation_event.for_saft_holders: Difference between SAFT purchase price and FMV of tokens at TGE may be ordinary income or capital gain depending on structure
tge_token_generation_event.for_founders_team: Tokens received for services are ordinary income at FMV when received (subject to vesting and potential 83(b) analysis if tokens are restricted)
tge_token_generation_event.lockup_periods: Tokens subject to contractual lockup may still be taxable at TGE if the taxpayer has dominion and control. IRS position on this is not fully settled.

non_taxable_events: Transferring between wallets owned by the same taxpayer
Gifting crypto (within annual exclusion limits; donor pays no tax, donee inherits basis)
Purchasing crypto with fiat currency (no gain/loss until disposition)

safes_with_token_side_letters.structure: Standard SAFE for equity rights plus separate token warrant or side letter granting rights to future tokens
safes_with_token_side_letters.tax_treatment: SAFE follows standard equity treatment. Token side letter creates a separate right — exercise/conversion at TGE is the taxable event.
safes_with_token_side_letters.trend: Increasingly standard for Web3 deals (2025 market preference over standalone SAFTs)

safts.structure: Pre-purchase agreement for tokens not yet minted. Investor pays upfront for right to receive tokens at TGE.
safts.tax_treatment: Generally treated as a prepaid forward contract. Tax triggered at TGE when tokens are delivered. Gain measured as FMV at delivery minus purchase price.
risks: Maturity dates may trigger repayment obligation if TGE does not occur
Valuation at TGE can be volatile and disputed

token_warrants.structure: Option to purchase tokens at set price, often expiring at TGE or specified date
token_warrants.tax_treatment: No tax at grant (if no readily ascertainable FMV). Tax at exercise on spread between exercise price and FMV of tokens received.
considerations: Multiple exercise provisions for multiple token launches
Allocation between equity and token components for tax purposes

open_questions_and_risks: IRS has not issued comprehensive guidance on DeFi-specific activities (LP positions, governance voting, protocol revenue sharing)
Treatment of wrapped tokens, bridges, and layer-2 transactions is unclear
Token lockups and vesting schedules — whether restricted tokens are taxable at TGE or at vesting is fact-dependent and not fully settled
DAO participation and token-based compensation structures lack specific guidance
Cross-border token distributions create complex sourcing and withholding questions
State tax treatment varies — some states have issued their own crypto guidance, others follow federal

cross_cutting_deal_flags.topic: Cross-Cutting Tax Flags for Deal Review
cross_cutting_deal_flags.summary: Tax issues that span multiple topics and should be checked in any deal.
flags: QSBS eligibility should be confirmed at every priced round (C-corp status, gross assets test, active business test)
83(b) filing status for all founders and early restricted stock recipients must be verified in diligence
409A valuation must be current before any option grants — verify date and trigger events since last valuation
Carried interest 3-year hold under Section 1061 affects GP economics and exit timing preferences
State tax exposure (especially California non-conformity with QSBS) can materially affect after-tax returns
Foreign investor participation requires CFIUS analysis for sensitive sectors and FIRPTA assessment
Token components (warrants, SAFTs, side letters) each create independent tax events that must be modeled separately
Management fee waiver programs at fund level are subject to Section 1061 scrutiny
Cap table secondary sales may break QSBS original issuance requirement
Change-in-control and acceleration provisions must be reviewed for 409A and 280G (golden parachute) compliance

internal_canonical_financing_context.purpose: Authoritative internal financing workflow context used as default operating guidance for drafting/review.

sections.rule: Start from economic terms first; escalate valuation, check size, option pool, board/control changes to the deal team.
operating_notes: If key terms are missing, gather only blocking missing fields and continue with explicit assumptions.
Economic changes are high-priority and require investment-team alignment.
Non-economic edits can be traded for speed/relationship goals if red lines are preserved.

sections.rule: Apply stronger Paradigm-specific control asks in early lead rounds and calibrated asks in late-stage minority positions.
operating_notes: Early lead + meaningful ownership: push for Paradigm-sensitive protective controls.
Late-stage small minority: bespoke veto ask may be less realistic; document leverage limits clearly.
Always preserve deterministic red lines even when posture is calibrated.

sections.rule: Token package treatment should be relevance-based: include robust token controls where token pathways are plausible; otherwise note rationale for omission.
operating_notes: If token relevance is unclear, default to explicit token-rights decisioning rather than silent omission.
Where included, ensure net exercise, lockup parity, and consent controls are coherent across docs.

sections.rule: Diligence is primarily cap-table tie-out plus IP ownership integrity, with pragmatic remediation over gotcha hunting.
operating_notes: For each equity issuance, require both approval evidence and signed issuance/grant evidence.
Resolve missing approvals or paper gaps before close via cleanup actions.
Prioritize practical remediation sequencing for founder/company success.

sections.rule: Review in this sequence: charter -> SPA -> IRA -> voting -> ROFR -> ancillaries; enforce cross-document consistency throughout.
operating_notes: Charter: verify term-sheet controls and deviations from baseline forms.
SPA: check reps/warranties set and schedule/purchaser integrity.
IRA/Voting/ROFR: confirm major investor rights, amendment mechanics, board/drag/transfer logic.

sections.rule: Use signature escrow discipline, confirm approvals and filed charter, then fund based on lead/following posture.
operating_notes: Hold signatures pending full approval/completeness conditions.
Lead investor typically wires first; followers can sequence after lead funding confirmation.
Require receipt confirmation and final closing evidence bundle.

internal_canonical_source_index.purpose: Canonical source map for highest-priority internal legal context.

sources.notes: Primary source for historical drafting patterns and negotiation posture calibration.
Primary source for diligence and definitive-document issue patterning.
Canonical checklist reference for financing process coverage.
Use for process framing and issue-spotting sequence.
Canonical operating context for legal-mode behavior and workflow sequencing.
Canonical structural and stylistic baseline for generated term-sheet artifacts.

internal_canonical_source_index.conflict_rule: If internal canonical sources conflict with generalized market summaries, prefer internal canonical sources and log the conflict.

corporate_operations_evergreen.purpose: Durable legal operations doctrine loaded by default for review-quality consistency.

sections.rule: Map every action through statute -> charter -> bylaws -> stockholder agreements before concluding authority.
checklist: Classify action type (issuance, amendment, merger, debt, related-party, board change).
Confirm no lower-level document conflicts with higher-level authority.
Track whether right is hard corporate-law veto or contractual covenant.

sections.rule: Every closing action must have explicit required approvals and threshold logic.
checklist: Board, stockholder, class/series, and contractual consent requirements mapped.
Threshold math validated against latest cap table.
Evidence bundle retained (signed consents + tally support).

sections.rule: Shared terms and governance/economic mechanics must reconcile across charter, SPA, IRA, voting, ROFR, and side letters.
checklist: Major Investor / Affiliate / Fully Diluted / Conversion Price definitions aligned.
Board composition and designation rights aligned.
Protective provision scope and amendment standards aligned.

venture_financing_operations.purpose: Deterministic close execution controls and document integrity checks.

document_stack_order.rule: Canonical order: charter -> SPA -> IRA -> voting -> ROFR -> ancillaries -> signature escrow -> close/fund.
Required docs by deal type are present and complete.

signature_packet_integrity.rule: Signature pages must match final document hashes.
All required signers/capacities collected and valid.

cap_table_tie_out.rule: Every issuance must tie to both approval and signed instrument.
Fully diluted math and investor allocations reconcile exactly.

secretary_certificate.rule: Mandatory bundle: charter, bylaws, resolutions, incumbency, specimen signatures.

employment_equity_ip_framework.purpose: Evergreen employment/equity/IP diligence controls with explicit dynamic-law boundaries.

items.rule: Signed confidentiality + inventions assignment coverage required for all founders, employees, and core contractors touching product/IP.

severity.RED_LINE: Core contributor missing assignment or chain-of-title break.

items.rule: Pre-incorporation and prior-employer overlap must be documented and remediated with releases/confirmatory assignments where needed.
Equity grants require valid authority + FMV support + grant-level paper trail tie-out.
83(b) deadline control and proof-of-filing retention are mandatory for restricted stock/early exercise workflows.
Local invention-assignment enforceability and worker-classification tests must be retrieved per jurisdiction.

crypto_context_split.purpose: Explicit split between crypto mechanics that are stable vs fast-changing regulatory posture.
system_evergreen: Token warrant mechanics (net exercise baseline, MFN framing, lockup mechanics taxonomy).
Cap table/token table parity framework and reconciliation checkpoints.
Governance rights placement discipline (charter vs agreements) and cross-doc consistency.
Unlock schedule stress-test controls and exception approval protocol.
lookup_dynamic: Current SEC/CFTC enforcement and rulemaking posture.
Recent court decisions affecting token classification/governance liability.
Jurisdiction-level updates (MiCA, UK FCA, MAS, sanctions/export controls).
